Labrador CMS.history: Labrador CMS was developed starting around 2007 as a joint venture between Aller Media and Egmont, two major Northern European publishers. It was formally launched as a standalone cloud-based CMS SaaS in 2016, led by CEO Jon Reidar Hammerfjeld, with roots in Dagbladet (one of Norway's largest newspapers). The company is headquartered in Oslo, Norway, and raised €2.5M in funding. It powers over 200 publisher sites across six countries including Elle, Dagbladet, and TV2.no.
Labrador CMS.summary_abuse: Labrador CMS is a purpose-built CMS for professional newsrooms and publishers, used by established media companies. As a B2B platform targeting professional journalists, it has a very low abuse profile. Content published through Labrador CMS is the responsibility of the publisher organizations using it. No notable security incidents or abuse campaigns documented.

Ladipage.history: LadiPage was founded in 2016 by Binh Nguyen and Tinh Nguyen, and is headquartered in Hanoi, Vietnam. It is a Vietnamese all-in-one marketing platform combining drag-and-drop landing page creation, website design, sales management, automation, and analytics. The platform has focused primarily on the Southeast Asian market, particularly Vietnamese businesses and online marketers.
Ladipage.summary_abuse: LadiPage is a landing page builder popular in Vietnam and Southeast Asia. Landing page platforms can be used to create phishing pages or fake promotional sites, and emerging-market landing page builders are sometimes associated with deceptive marketing campaigns. The platform enables users to host content including e-commerce stores and lead capture forms. No specific major abuse incidents documented for LadiPage.

Laga Widget.history: Laga Widget is a Swedish SaaS tool for car dismantlers to search and manage auto parts inventory. No notable history events documented.
Laga Widget.summary_abuse: Laga Widget is a niche B2B inventory management tool for auto dismantlers with no notable abuse signal.

Lagoon.history: Lagoon is an open-source application delivery platform for Kubernetes, created by amazee.io, a Swiss-origin web hosting company. Launched around 2017, Lagoon abstracts Kubernetes complexity for web developers and supports Drupal, WordPress, Node.js, and other web applications. Mirantis, the Kubernetes-focused cloud company, acquired amazee.io in 2022, bringing Lagoon under its portfolio. Lagoon is available as open source (Apache 2.0) and as a managed service.
Lagoon.summary_abuse: Lagoon is an infrastructure-level PaaS platform for hosting web applications on Kubernetes. As a developer tool for running code on your own infrastructure, it has minimal direct abuse surface. No notable abuse incidents documented. Customers using the managed service by Mirantis/amazee.io benefit from professional infrastructure operations.

Landbot.history: Landbot was founded in 2017 in Barcelona, Spain, by Cristobal Villar and Jiaqi Pan. The company raised VC funding and quickly gained traction in the no-code conversational marketing space. Landbot allows businesses to create chatbot-driven landing pages, surveys, and lead generation flows without coding. It has positioned itself as a user-friendly alternative to more developer-focused chatbot platforms.
Landbot.summary_abuse: Landbot is a no-code chatbot and conversational landing page builder. As with any platform enabling users to create interactive forms and collect visitor data, it could theoretically be used to build phishing flows or deceptive lead capture pages. However, Landbot operates under Spanish and EU regulations, requiring GDPR compliance for data collected. No specific widespread abuse of Landbot infrastructure documented.

Landingi.history: Landingi was founded in 2012 in Poland as a landing page creation and optimization platform. The company grew to serve thousands of businesses with drag-and-drop landing page building, A/B testing, and lead management features. As a Polish company it operates under EU/GDPR regulations. It has expanded internationally and competes with Unbounce, Instapage, and similar tools.
Landingi.summary_abuse: Landingi is a landing page builder that allows users to host pages on custom domains or on Landingi subdomains. Like any hosted page builder, it could be used to create deceptive pages or phishing sites. The platform allows users to collect form submissions, which could include sensitive data. Operating under Polish/EU law provides GDPR accountability. No specific large-scale abuse campaigns documented.

LandingPress.history: LandingPress is a WordPress landing page theme distributed as a commercial product. No notable ownership or history events documented.
LandingPress.summary_abuse: LandingPress is a WordPress landing page theme. Like any page builder theme, it could be used to create fake landing pages, but no specific abuse incidents have been documented.

langify.history: langify is a Shopify translation app launched in 2014 by langify GmbH & Co. KG that enables merchants to offer their stores in multiple languages. It is a specialist Shopify ecosystem tool with no notable ownership changes.
langify.summary_abuse: langify is a Shopify translation app with no notable abuse signal. It operates within the Shopify ecosystem and is used exclusively by merchants for store localization, with no consumer-facing content hosting or user-generated features that would attract fraudsters.

LangShop.history: LangShop is a Shopify translation app that enables merchants to translate their store content into multiple languages to reach international customers. Founded around 2019, the app is available on the Shopify App Store and targets e-commerce businesses looking to expand to non-English speaking markets.
LangShop.summary_abuse: LangShop is a Shopify translation utility with a B2B focus. It processes store content for translation but does not handle payment data or host user-generated content at scale. Low abuse risk profile for a narrow-purpose Shopify integration. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Lapis.history: Lapis is an enterprise ecommerce solutions provider. No notable history or acquisition events documented.
Lapis.summary_abuse: Lapis is an ecommerce SaaS with no notable abuse signal documented.

Laravel.history: Laravel was created by Taylor Otwell and first released in June 2011 as an open-source PHP web framework. It was designed to provide a more elegant and expressive alternative to the then-dominant CodeIgniter framework. Laravel introduced features like Eloquent ORM, Blade templating, Artisan CLI, and built-in authentication that quickly made it the most popular PHP framework by 2015-2016. Taylor Otwell founded Laravel LLC to support commercial products around the ecosystem including Laravel Forge, Vapor, Nova, and Envoyer.
Laravel.summary_abuse: Laravel is the dominant PHP web framework used to build millions of legitimate web applications. As an open-source framework, it is used across the full spectrum of web development including by scammers and phishing kit developers, but the overwhelming use is legitimate. Laravel itself is backend infrastructure and has no direct consumer surface; abuse occurs at the application layer built on top of it, not the framework itself. Taylor Otwell and the core team maintain a responsible disclosure policy.

Laravel Echo.history: Laravel Echo is a JavaScript library created as part of the Laravel ecosystem by Taylor Otwell and released around 2015-2016. It provides an expressive API for subscribing to WebSocket channels and listening for server-side broadcast events, designed to work with Laravel's broadcasting feature and backends like Pusher or Laravel Reverb. Released as MIT open source.
Laravel Echo.summary_abuse: Laravel Echo is a client-side JavaScript library for real-time event broadcasting. As a foundational open-source library used in legitimate Laravel applications, it has no meaningful direct abuse surface. No known abuse patterns or security incidents specific to the library.

Lasso CRM.history: Lasso CRM is a specialized CRM platform for new home builders, real estate developers, and new home sales agencies, founded in Canada around 2002. It focuses specifically on the new construction and real estate sales pipeline, tracking leads, site traffic, and home sales. The platform has become one of the established niche CRM providers in the North American residential real estate development industry.
Lasso CRM.summary_abuse: Lasso CRM is a specialized B2B CRM platform serving the new home real estate industry. As an enterprise CRM with vertical focus and professional customers, its abuse risk is low. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

Laterpay.history: LaterPay was founded in 2010 in Munich, Germany, as a micropayment platform allowing publishers to offer digital content on a pay-later model where users can access content immediately and pay when their accumulated total reaches a threshold. The company received VC backing and partnered with major German media publishers. LaterPay expanded its model to include subscriptions and time passes. The company has focused on the media and digital content monetization space.
Laterpay.summary_abuse: LaterPay is a payment platform for digital content publishers. As a payment processor, it handles financial transactions and is subject to payment card industry standards. The deferred payment model (buy now, pay later for digital content) has theoretical risk of users accumulating charges they may dispute, but LaterPay has built-in spending limits. No notable widespread fraud or abuse incidents documented.

LatitudePay.history: LatitudePay is a buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) product launched in 2019 by Latitude Financial Services, an Australian consumer finance company. Latitude Financial was formerly the GE Capital consumer finance arm in Australia and New Zealand. LatitudePay offered interest-free instalments for retail purchases in Australia and Singapore. Latitude Financial went public on the ASX in 2021. In 2023, Latitude suffered a significant data breach exposing approximately 14 million customer records.
LatitudePay.summary_abuse: LatitudePay/Latitude Financial suffered a major data breach in March 2023 that exposed approximately 14 million customer records including passport numbers, driver's licenses, and financial information. This incident significantly impacted trust in the platform. BNPL services generally carry risk from fraudulent account creation using stolen identity documents. Latitude has since enhanced its security posture following the breach.

LaunchDarkly.history: LaunchDarkly was founded in 2014 by Edith Harbaugh and John Kodumal in Oakland, California. It pioneered feature flag management as a service and became the market leader in the feature management space. The company raised over $350M in venture capital at a $3B valuation by 2022. LaunchDarkly served major enterprises including IBM, Atlassian, and Microsoft. It expanded from feature flags to a full continuous delivery platform with experimentation and monitoring.
LaunchDarkly.summary_abuse: LaunchDarkly is a developer infrastructure platform for feature flag management used by enterprise engineering teams. It processes event telemetry from applications using its SDK, which represents user interaction data. The platform is SOC 2 Type 2 certified, GDPR compliant, HIPAA capable, and maintains a public bug bounty program. As a backend developer tool, it has a very low consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable abuse incidents documented.

LaunchNotes.history: LaunchNotes was founded in 2020 as a product changelog and release communications platform. It enables product teams to publish feature announcements, keep stakeholders aligned, and gather user feedback on releases. The platform helps bridge the gap between engineering releases and customer communication in product-led growth companies.
LaunchNotes.summary_abuse: LaunchNotes is a B2B product changelog platform used by software teams. As a platform for publishing product release notes and gathering user feedback, it has minimal abuse surface. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

Launchrock.history: Launchrock was founded around 2011 as a landing page platform specifically designed for startup launch campaigns and pre-launch email collection. It gained popularity during the startup boom as an easy way to build "coming soon" pages with viral referral mechanics. The service was acquired by Sievert Larson, founder of TypePad, and changed ownership multiple times. Activity has significantly declined since its early 2010s peak.
Launchrock.summary_abuse: Launchrock is a landing page platform that allows users to create email capture pages on Launchrock-hosted URLs. Such platforms are occasionally used to create fake product launch pages or phishing email collection sites. The platform's reduced activity and maintenance suggest limited ongoing abuse monitoring. No specific large incidents documented.

Lawmatics.history: Lawmatics was founded in 2018 in San Diego, California, as a legal CRM and marketing automation platform specifically for law firms. It focuses on client intake, lead tracking, marketing automation, and workflow management for attorneys. The company raised VC funding and positioned itself in the legal tech space as an alternative to generic CRMs for solo and small law firm attorneys.
Lawmatics.summary_abuse: Lawmatics is a CRM platform for law firms that processes sensitive client intake data including personal information, case details, and sometimes financial information. Law firm software handles highly sensitive attorney-client privileged information. As a legal tech platform serving professional attorneys, it has a low consumer-facing abuse profile. No notable security incidents documented.

LawPay.history: LawPay was founded in 2009 and is owned by AffiniPay, a payment processing company specializing in professional services. LawPay is purpose-built for law firms, designed specifically to comply with IOLTA trust accounting rules that govern how attorneys handle client funds. It is endorsed by over 60 state and local bar associations in the US. The platform processes billions of dollars in legal payments annually.
LawPay.summary_abuse: LawPay is a specialized payment processor for law firms that is endorsed by bar associations and required to meet trust account compliance rules (IOLTA). As a regulated payment processor with professional customers, it maintains PCI-DSS compliance and SOC 2 certification. Payment processors are potential targets for fraud, but LawPay's professional clientele and regulatory context reduce risk. No notable large-scale fraud incidents documented.

LayBuy.history: Laybuy was founded in 2016 in New Zealand by Gary and Alex Rohloff as a buy-now-pay-later solution allowing consumers to split purchases into 6 weekly interest-free payments. The company expanded from New Zealand and Australia to the UK market. Laybuy faced financial difficulties in 2023 and entered receivership in New Zealand, with UK operations continuing separately. The company exemplifies the challenging economics of the BNPL sector.
LayBuy.summary_abuse: Laybuy is a BNPL provider that has faced financial difficulty entering receivership in New Zealand in 2023. BNPL services carry inherent risk of consumers accumulating debt they cannot repay. Fraudulent account creation using stolen identity details is a known risk for BNPL platforms with minimal KYC. No specific major fraud incidents documented against Laybuy specifically.

Layers.history: Layers is a site search and merchandising platform designed for ecommerce businesses, helping retailers improve product discovery through advanced search, filtering, and merchandising rules. It competes in the ecommerce search market alongside tools like Algolia and Searchanise.
Layers.summary_abuse: Layers is a B2B ecommerce search and merchandising platform. As an infrastructure tool integrated into merchant websites for search functionality, it has a low consumer-facing abuse profile. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

Laylo.history: Laylo was founded around 2020 as a fan engagement and drop notification CRM platform targeting musicians, creators, and artists. It allows creators to notify fans via SMS, email, or social platforms when new merchandise, tour tickets, or content drops become available. The platform grew alongside the creator economy and has been used by various artists to build direct fan relationships.
Laylo.summary_abuse: Laylo is a fan notification and drop CRM platform for artists and creators. It collects consumer contact information (phone numbers, emails) on behalf of artists for fan notification campaigns. Like any SMS/email platform, it could be used for spam if misused, but its creator-focused positioning reduces general abuse risk. No notable security incidents documented.

LayoutHub.history: LayoutHub is a Shopify page builder app that provides a library of pre-designed layouts and blocks for merchants to quickly create storefronts, landing pages, and product pages. Founded around 2020, likely by a Vietnamese development team, it is available on the Shopify App Store and competes with PageFly, GemPages, and similar Shopify page building tools.
LayoutHub.summary_abuse: LayoutHub is a Shopify page builder app used by merchants to design store pages. As a B2B Shopify tool, its abuse surface is limited to merchants potentially using it to create deceptive store pages, which would be governed by Shopify's terms. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

Layui.history: Layui is a Chinese open-source modular front-end UI component library first released around 2016 by Xian Sheng (贤心). It became popular in China for building web admin panels and backend interfaces with a clean, lightweight aesthetic. Layui uses a module-based approach where components are loaded on demand. The library is particularly popular in Chinese enterprise web development and government projects.
Layui.summary_abuse: Layui is an open-source UI component library primarily used in Chinese backend/admin panel development. As a pure front-end library with no server-side component, it has no direct abuse surface beyond what applications built with it may do. No known security vulnerabilities or abuse patterns specific to Layui.

LayUp.history: LayUp is a South African payment technology platform founded around 2018 that enables customers to pay for goods and services in installments over time. It focuses on the South African retail market, offering a layaway/installment payment option for merchants.
LayUp.summary_abuse: LayUp is a South African installment payment platform primarily serving local merchants. As a payment technology with a focus on the domestic South African market, it has a limited international footprint. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

Lazada.history: Lazada was founded in 2012 by Rocket Internet in Singapore as a B2B2C marketplace for Southeast Asia. In 2016, Alibaba Group acquired a controlling stake for $1 billion, making it Alibaba's primary Southeast Asian marketplace. Alibaba subsequently acquired full ownership. Lazada operates in Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam and is one of the largest ecommerce platforms in the region.
Lazada.summary_abuse: Lazada is a major Southeast Asian ecommerce marketplace with documented issues around counterfeit goods, fake reviews, and seller fraud common to large open marketplaces. As an Alibaba subsidiary, it shares some characteristics with AliExpress regarding third-party seller quality control challenges. Consumer complaints about delayed shipping, wrong products, and difficult returns are common. Lazada operates under various Southeast Asian regulatory frameworks and has AML/KYC for payment processing.

LazySizes.history: LazySizes is an open-source JavaScript library created by Alexander Farkas (aFarkas) around 2014 for lazy loading images, iframes, and other media elements only when they enter the viewport. It became one of the most popular lazy loading solutions before native browser lazy loading (loading='lazy') became widely supported in 2019-2020. The library is available on GitHub under MIT license.
LazySizes.summary_abuse: LazySizes is a front-end performance library for lazy loading media. As a pure JavaScript utility library with no server-side component or external data collection, it has no meaningful abuse surface. No known security vulnerabilities or abuse patterns.

LazySizes unveilhooks plugin.history: The LazySizes unveilhooks plugin is an extension to the LazySizes lazy loading library, created by Alexander Farkas (aFarkas). It extends LazySizes to support lazy loading of scripts/widgets, background images, CSS styles, and video/audio elements beyond just standard img tags. Part of the same open-source MIT-licensed project as LazySizes itself.
LazySizes unveilhooks plugin.summary_abuse: The LazySizes unveilhooks plugin is an open-source extension to a performance library. As a pure front-end JavaScript utility with no server-side component, it has no meaningful abuse surface. No known security vulnerabilities or abuse patterns.

Lead Concept.history: Lead Concept is a French lead generation marketing automation platform. No notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Lead Concept.summary_abuse: Lead Concept is a small lead generation platform. Lead gen platforms can be used for spam, but no specific abuse incidents have been documented for this service.

Lead Forensics.history: Lead Forensics was founded in 2009 in Portsmouth, UK, as a B2B website visitor identification tool that cross-references IP addresses against a proprietary database to reveal which companies visited a website. It has raised significant revenue and expanded internationally. The company has been controversial in privacy circles due to its core function of identifying individuals behind anonymous website visits by company IP, creating significant GDPR scrutiny in the EU.
Lead Forensics.summary_abuse: Lead Forensics is inherently privacy-invasive by design: it identifies the companies behind anonymous website visits using IP-to-company database matching. Privacy regulators and GDPR practitioners have questioned its legal basis for processing data of website visitors without consent. While technically B2B intelligence, website visitors are not informed their company identity is being captured. The UK ICO has received complaints about similar services. Lead Forensics claims GDPR compliance but the underlying product design is controversial from a privacy standpoint.

Lead Generated.history: Lead Generated is a marketing CRM platform centralizing customer data and automating campaigns. No notable history events documented.
Lead Generated.summary_abuse: Lead Generated is a marketing CRM with no notable abuse signal. CRM and marketing automation platforms have inherent spam risk but no specific abuse pattern documented.

Lead Prosper.history: Lead Prosper is a US-based lead distribution platform founded around 2019 that facilitates real-time lead routing between lead suppliers (affiliate marketers, publishers) and lead buyers (insurance companies, financial services, home services). It competes in the performance marketing lead distribution space.
Lead Prosper.summary_abuse: Lead Prosper is a lead distribution platform operating in the performance marketing space, facilitating connections between lead generators and buyers. Lead distribution platforms are central to the broader lead generation industry, which has known issues with low-quality, recycled, or fraudulently generated leads being sold repeatedly to multiple buyers. The insurance and financial services lead buying industry has faced regulatory scrutiny over telemarketing abuse. No specific incidents documented for Lead Prosper itself.

Lead-Finity Webchat.history: Lead-Finity Webchat is a lead conversion live chat platform. No notable history documented.
Lead-Finity Webchat.summary_abuse: Lead-Finity Webchat is a small lead conversion chat tool with no notable abuse signal documented.

Leadbit.history: Leadbit is an offer-based advertising network that targets users with ads based on engagement and behavioral profiles. It operates in the performance marketing and affiliate advertising space, running global ad campaigns across health, finance, and nutraceutical verticals.
Leadbit.summary_abuse: Leadbit operates in the performance marketing advertising space, serving behavioral-targeted ads across affiliate networks. Performance ad networks focusing on nutraceutical, health, and financial offers are frequently associated with deceptive advertising practices, fake urgency, and misleading health claims. The ad-based network model with many independent publishers creates challenges for quality control. No specific major regulatory actions documented against Leadbit, but the vertical carries elevated abuse risk.

LeadBoxer.history: LeadBoxer was founded in 2014 in Amsterdam, Netherlands, as a B2B lead generation and website visitor identification platform. Similar to Lead Forensics, it uses IP-to-company database matching and behavioral tracking to identify website visitors and score leads. Operating from the Netherlands places it squarely under GDPR jurisdiction.
LeadBoxer.summary_abuse: LeadBoxer uses website tracking to identify companies behind anonymous website visits using IP address databases, similar to Lead Forensics. This form of reverse IP lookup without visitor consent is legally contentious under GDPR. The platform collects behavioral data from site visitors who have not explicitly consented to company-level identification. The Dutch DPA (AP) has been active in GDPR enforcement. LeadBoxer claims GDPR compliance but the core data collection practice is contested.

LeadByte.history: LeadByte is a UK-based lead management platform founded around 2014, providing real-time lead capture, validation, and distribution for performance marketing and affiliate networks. It targets businesses in insurance, financial services, and home improvement that buy and sell leads through affiliate channels. The platform is ICO registered and operates under UK data protection law.
LeadByte.summary_abuse: LeadByte operates in the lead distribution market, facilitating sales of consumer leads between publishers and buyers. The lead generation industry has documented issues with fraudulent leads, TCPA violations (in US contexts), and leads being sold to multiple buyers simultaneously. UK-based operations are subject to ICO enforcement and GDPR. No specific publicly reported LeadByte incidents, but the sector carries inherent risk.

LeadChat.history: LeadChat is an Australian live chat service provider founded around 2014 that combines LiveChat software with live human operators to respond to website visitors on behalf of client businesses. The service targets businesses that want to convert website traffic but lack in-house staff for 24/7 chat coverage.
LeadChat.summary_abuse: LeadChat provides managed live chat services using human operators for client websites. As a chat outsourcing service, it processes visitor conversations that may include personal and sensitive business inquiries. The service collects contact information on behalf of clients. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented.

LeadDyno.history: LeadDyno was founded in 2012 in the US as an affiliate marketing and tracking platform. It allows businesses to set up and manage affiliate programs, track referrals, and pay commissions to affiliates. It integrates with Shopify, WooCommerce, and other ecommerce platforms and has served thousands of businesses with self-managed affiliate programs.
LeadDyno.summary_abuse: LeadDyno is an affiliate program management platform. Affiliate networks and self-managed affiliate programs can attract abusive affiliates who use spam, cookie stuffing, or fake traffic to generate fraudulent commissions. The platform provides the infrastructure for managing these programs, with the responsibility for affiliate quality resting with the merchant. No specific major fraud incidents documented for LeadDyno infrastructure.

Leadfeeder.history: Leadfeeder was founded in 2012 in Helsinki, Finland, as a B2B website visitor identification tool. It uses Google Analytics data and IP-to-company databases to identify which companies visit a website. The company merged with Echobot in 2022 to form Dealfront, a B2B sales intelligence company. Leadfeeder continues as a product brand within the Dealfront portfolio.
Leadfeeder.summary_abuse: Leadfeeder identifies companies behind anonymous website visits using IP address databases, which is legally contentious under GDPR without visitor consent. Finnish DPA and the broader EDPB have scrutinized IP-based company identification services. Leadfeeder claims GDPR compliance but the core data practice (company-level identification without consent) is contested. As a Finnish company now operating as part of Dealfront, it operates under EU jurisdiction.

Leadferno.history: Leadferno is a US-based conversion and messaging platform founded around 2019 that helps businesses capture more website leads through text messaging and click-to-text functionality. It targets local service businesses, allowing them to text with website visitors.
Leadferno.summary_abuse: Leadferno is a lead capture and text messaging platform for local businesses. It processes phone numbers and enables businesses to text with prospects. SMS-based platforms carry risk of being used for spam or unwanted marketing messages. No specific notable abuse incidents documented.

Leadific.history: Leadific is a CRM platform for digital agencies to manage clients, communications, and workflows. It appears to be a white-label or rebrand of a broader CRM platform, targeting digital marketing agencies as a resellable client management solution.
Leadific.summary_abuse: Leadific is a CRM platform for digital agencies. As a B2B CRM tool with no consumer-facing hosting features, it presents minimal abuse risk. No notable security incidents documented.

LEADIN.history: LEADIN was HubSpot's free lead capture WordPress plugin, created to help small businesses capture leads and connect them to HubSpot's CRM. HubSpot was founded in 2006 by Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah at MIT and went public on NYSE in 2014. LEADIN served as a free entry point to the HubSpot ecosystem for WordPress site owners. The plugin has been integrated more deeply into HubSpot's broader free tools suite over time.
LEADIN.summary_abuse: LEADIN is a HubSpot-owned lead capture plugin backed by a large, publicly-traded US company with strong security and compliance posture. HubSpot maintains SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001, GDPR DPA, and a public bug bounty program. Lead capture tools inherently collect visitor data and email addresses, but HubSpot's well-established privacy practices and transparent policies reduce abuse risk. No notable abuse incidents specific to LEADIN.

Leadinfo.history: Leadinfo was founded in 2015 in the Netherlands as a B2B lead generation tool that identifies which companies visit a website using IP-to-company database matching and third-party data enrichment. It competes with Leadfeeder and Lead Forensics in the European B2B website visitor identification market, operating under Dutch/EU jurisdiction and GDPR.
Leadinfo.summary_abuse: Leadinfo identifies companies behind anonymous website visits without explicit visitor consent, which is legally contentious under GDPR. The Dutch DPA (AP) actively enforces GDPR and has scrutinized similar services. Leadinfo claims GDPR compliance through legitimate interest legal basis, but this is contested by privacy regulators. Similar to Leadfeeder and Lead Forensics, the core product design involves tracking individuals at the company level without their knowledge.

LeadPages.history: Leadpages was founded in 2012 by Clay Collins in Minneapolis, MN, as a landing page builder for small businesses and online marketers. It grew to become one of the leading landing page platforms with hundreds of thousands of users. The company raised venture capital including a $38M Series B round. Leadpages expanded from landing pages to include pop-ups, alert bars, and site building functionality.
LeadPages.summary_abuse: Leadpages is a landing page builder that allows users to host pages on custom domains or Leadpages-hosted subdomains. The platform has been used to create deceptive landing pages for scams, fake investment promotions, and phishing campaigns. As a widely-used hosted page builder, Leadpages pages appear in phishing URL feeds. The company has an AUP and abuse reporting mechanism but takedown speed varies. The combination of Leadpages hosting with custom domain support makes it a recurring abuse vector.

Leads2b.history: Leads2b was founded around 2018 as a Brazilian B2B sales prospecting platform that helps identify and access company data from Brazil for sales, marketing, and business development. It focuses on the Brazilian market, providing access to company databases for generating qualified leads. The platform operates under Brazilian LGPD (Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados).
Leads2b.summary_abuse: Leads2b is a B2B prospecting and company data platform for the Brazilian market. It provides access to company contact databases for cold outreach. Sales prospecting platforms that provide bulk contact data can facilitate unwanted cold calls and emails, though B2B outreach is generally treated differently from consumer spam in Brazil's LGPD framework. No specific abuse incidents documented.

LeadsBridge.history: LeadsBridge was founded in 2014 in Italy as an integration platform (iPaaS) specifically for advertising and marketing teams. It specializes in connecting ad platforms like Facebook Lead Ads, LinkedIn, TikTok, and Google with CRMs and email marketing tools to automate lead syncing in real-time. The platform is an official Meta Business Partner and TikTok Partner.
LeadsBridge.summary_abuse: LeadsBridge is an integration platform for connecting advertising lead forms to CRMs. It processes leads captured from paid social media advertising campaigns on behalf of businesses. The platform itself does not generate leads but routes them between systems. Being an official Meta Partner means it adheres to Meta's data use policies for lead data. No specific abuse incidents documented for the platform itself.

LeadScore.history: LeadScore is an inbound marketing automation platform. No notable ownership events documented.
LeadScore.summary_abuse: LeadScore is a marketing automation platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

LeadSimple.history: LeadSimple was founded around 2016 as a CRM and sales automation platform specifically for property managers. It enables property management companies to automate their sales pipeline, client communications, and operational workflows. The platform targets the residential property management industry in North America.
LeadSimple.summary_abuse: LeadSimple is a vertical CRM platform for property managers. As a specialized B2B tool serving professional property management companies, it has a low consumer-facing abuse profile. It processes tenant and owner contact information. No notable security incidents documented.

LeadsLeap.history: LeadsLeap was founded around 2008 and operates as a combined lead generation, traffic exchange, and affiliate marketing system. It combines an ad network, viral marketing tools, and list management for online marketers, particularly those in the MMO (make money online) space. The platform operates on a traffic exchange model common in the old-school online marketing world.
LeadsLeap.summary_abuse: LeadsLeap operates in the traffic exchange and MMO affiliate marketing space, which is historically associated with low-quality traffic, inflated click counts, and pyramid-style referral schemes. Traffic exchange networks generate bot-like traffic patterns and often serve as lead generation for dubious opportunities. The platform's target audience in the make-money-online niche is frequently associated with deceptive marketing practices. No specific regulatory actions documented, but the business model carries elevated abuse risk.

LeadSlide.history: LeadSlide is a WordPress and ecommerce marketing campaign tool. Its website redirects to v1, suggesting limited active development.
LeadSlide.summary_abuse: LeadSlide is a small marketing campaign tool with no notable abuse signal documented.

Leadspace.history: Leadspace was founded in 2007 in San Francisco, CA, and Tel Aviv, Israel, as a B2B customer data platform (CDP) using AI to build comprehensive account and buyer profiles for sales and marketing teams. The company raised significant VC funding. Leadspace provides data enrichment, account scoring, and audience targeting for enterprise B2B companies, competing with Clearbit, ZoomInfo, and similar data enrichment platforms.
Leadspace.summary_abuse: Leadspace is a B2B data enrichment and CDP platform that aggregates third-party data on companies and business professionals. Like all data enrichment services, it processes publicly available and licensed data about individuals in professional contexts. B2B data platforms have faced GDPR scrutiny regarding individual professional contact data, though B2B data is generally treated with more latitude than consumer PII. No specific public incidents documented.

Leadsquared.history: LeadSquared was founded in 2011 in Bangalore, India, by Nilesh Patel as a marketing automation and CRM platform. The company raised significant VC funding and grew to serve thousands of businesses primarily in India and Southeast Asia, with strong vertical focus on education, healthcare, and financial services. LeadSquared achieved unicorn status ($1B+ valuation) in 2022 following a large funding round.
Leadsquared.summary_abuse: LeadSquared is a major Indian marketing automation and CRM platform serving thousands of enterprises. As a mature platform with SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA compliance, it has strong security posture for an Indian tech company. The platform processes substantial customer data for lead management and marketing automation. No major security incidents documented. Its enterprise focus reduces abuse risk compared to SMB-focused tools.

LeadsSight.history: LeadsSight is a visitor tracking and lead recovery tool that converts lost website visitors into leads. No notable history documented.
LeadsSight.summary_abuse: LeadsSight tracks website visitor behavior to identify anonymous users. Visitor tracking services raise privacy concerns and the data collection could be used to build marketing profiles, but no specific fraud abuse has been documented.

Leadster.history: Leadster is a Brazilian conversational marketing and chatbot lead generation platform founded around 2019. It enables businesses to engage website visitors with intelligent chatbot conversations to capture qualified leads. The platform is focused on the Brazilian market and operates under Brazil's LGPD data protection law.
Leadster.summary_abuse: Leadster is a Brazilian chatbot lead generation platform. It collects visitor information through conversational flows on behalf of client websites. As a B2B platform operating in Brazil under LGPD, it has moderate privacy risk typical of lead generation tools. No notable security incidents documented.

Leadtex.history: Leadtex (leadteh.ru) is a Russian chatbot development platform. No notable ownership events documented.
Leadtex.summary_abuse: Leadtex is a small Russian chatbot platform with no notable abuse signal. Being Russia-based raises general data residency considerations.

Leady.history: Leady is a Czech lead generation and customer intelligence platform that identifies anonymous website visitors by company. No notable ownership events documented.
Leady.summary_abuse: Leady identifies anonymous website visitors and collects company data, which raises privacy considerations. As a B2B lead intelligence tool it is not directly associated with consumer fraud, but GDPR compliance for visitor identification is a concern.

Leaflet.history: Leaflet is an open-source JavaScript library for mobile-friendly interactive maps, created by Vladimir Agafonkin and first released in 2011. It quickly became the industry-standard lightweight map library, favored for its simplicity and small footprint compared to heavier alternatives like Google Maps API or OpenLayers. Maintained by a community of contributors, Leaflet is used by major organizations including Wikipedia, GitHub, and government agencies worldwide.
Leaflet.summary_abuse: Leaflet is a foundational open-source JavaScript mapping library with no server-side component or data collection. As a pure client-side rendering library, it has no direct abuse surface. Maps powered by Leaflet may display any tile source, but the library itself does not host content. No known security vulnerabilities or abuse patterns.

Leaflet platform.history: Leaflet platform (in Wappalyzer's A/B Testing category) is a price testing and optimization platform for Shopify stores, separate from the Leaflet.js open-source mapping library. It allows merchants to run pricing experiments and optimize revenue. Founded around 2020 as a Shopify app.
Leaflet platform.summary_abuse: Leaflet platform is a Shopify pricing optimization app. As a B2B Shopify tool running price experiments for merchants, it has a very low consumer-facing abuse surface. No notable security incidents documented.

Leafly.history: Leafly was founded in 2010 in Seattle, WA, by Scott Vickers, Brian Wansolich, and Cy Scott as a cannabis strain information and dispensary finder platform. It went public via SPAC merger in 2021 on the NASDAQ (LFLY). Leafly operates the world's largest cannabis marketplace connecting consumers with dispensaries and brands. The company has faced financial challenges in the post-SPAC period with declining stock price.
Leafly.summary_abuse: Leafly is a cannabis information and marketplace platform operating in a regulated industry. Age verification for cannabis-related platforms is a legal requirement in most US states. Leafly collects user data for dispensary recommendations and has faced scrutiny over data privacy in the sensitive context of cannabis use records. Some cannabis delivery services accessed through Leafly have operated in regulatory gray areas. The platform is publicly listed and subject to SEC reporting requirements.

Leaky Paywall.history: Leaky Paywall is a WordPress plugin for implementing metered paywalls on WordPress-based publications, allowing site owners to give readers a set number of free article views before requiring a subscription. It was developed as an open-source solution for publishers wanting a New York Times-style metered paywall without enterprise paywall costs. Available on the WordPress plugin repository and as a premium version.
Leaky Paywall.summary_abuse: Leaky Paywall is a self-hosted WordPress plugin for content gating. As a plugin that runs on the customer's own infrastructure with no centralized hosting, it has no direct abuse surface at the vendor level. The plugin itself collects minimal data about page views. No notable security incidents documented.

LeanData.history: LeanData was founded in 2012 in Santa Clara, CA, by Evan Liang and Kelvin Cheung. The company specializes in lead-to-account matching and revenue operations automation for B2B Salesforce users. It raised over $90M in venture funding and became a key tool for enterprise marketing operations teams managing complex lead routing workflows.
LeanData.summary_abuse: LeanData is a revenue operations platform for enterprise B2B teams, deeply integrated with Salesforce. As an enterprise tool focused on internal lead routing and CRM data management, it has a very low consumer-facing abuse surface. SOC 2 Type 2 certified. No notable security incidents documented.

Leanplum.history: Leanplum was founded in 2012 in San Francisco by Momchil Kyurkchiev, Andrew First, and Kasper Lund as a mobile marketing automation and A/B testing platform. The company raised over $90M in venture capital and served enterprise mobile app companies with push notifications, in-app messaging, and personalization. In 2022, Leanplum was acquired by CleverTap, an India-based mobile marketing platform, and merged into CleverTap's product.
Leanplum.summary_abuse: Leanplum is an enterprise mobile marketing and A/B testing platform now part of CleverTap. It processes mobile user behavioral data to power personalized in-app messages and push notifications. As an enterprise tool with SOC 2 certification and GDPR compliance, its security posture is solid. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented for the platform.

Learnbase.history: Learnbase is an AI-powered LMS for content creators and students. No notable ownership events documented.
Learnbase.summary_abuse: Learnbase is a learning management system with no notable abuse signal.

LearnDash.history: LearnDash was founded in 2012 as a WordPress LMS (Learning Management System) plugin, becoming one of the most popular platforms for creating online courses on WordPress. The plugin gained adoption by universities, corporations, and course creators. In 2020, LearnDash was acquired by Liquid Web, a managed hosting company, which has supported continued development. LearnDash is used by major institutions including the University of Michigan and Fortune 500 companies.
LearnDash.summary_abuse: LearnDash is a self-hosted WordPress LMS plugin used by legitimate educational institutions and course creators. As a product that runs on the customer's own infrastructure, it has no centralized hosting abuse surface. The GPL-licensed codebase is well-reviewed by the WordPress community. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented. Backed by the established managed hosting company Liquid Web.

LearnUpon.history: Founded 2012 in Dublin by Brendan Noud and Des Anderson. Raised $56.65M total, backed by Summit Partners and Enterprise Ireland. Acquired AI course-creation platform Courseau in November 2025. Approximately 287 employees as of 2026.
LearnUpon.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LearnUpon is a B2B SaaS LMS for corporate training with no known data misuse incidents. It does not host arbitrary public content or expose mass-messaging features. SOC 2 Type 2 and ISO 27701 certified, GDPR compliant with DPA available. Vendor has a responsible disclosure policy on their security page.

LearnWorlds.history: Founded 2014 in Greece by Fanis Despotakis, George Palegeorgiou, and Panos Siozos. Raised $33.11M total including a Series A from Insight Partners and Marathon Venture Capital. Headquarters moved to London; also has offices in Cyprus and Greece. Around 140-172 employees as of 2025.
LearnWorlds.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B SaaS platform for creating and selling online courses. Does not host arbitrary public content under custom domains beyond course schools. GDPR compliant with a toolkit for school administrators. PCI-DSS compliant for payment handling. No known major abuse incidents reported.

LearnyBox.history: LearnyBox is a French all-in-one platform for online course creation and training, serving over 10,000 coaches and trainers. Founded circa 2014 in France. Customers have generated over 600 million euros in revenue. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes on record.
LearnyBox.summary_abuse: Low abuse risk. French B2B SaaS LMS for online entrepreneurs and training organisations. GDPR compliant given French regulatory environment. Primarily serves course creators and trainers. No known notable abuse incidents.

LeaseHawk.history: Founded 2012 in Scottsdale, Arizona by Mike Mueller, who acquired a telephony platform from CallSource. Acquired by MRI Software in August 2021 and integrated into MRI's real estate software suite. Specialises in AI-powered CRM for multifamily property leasing.
LeaseHawk.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B property management CRM used by multifamily real estate operators. No consumer-facing surface and no known data misuse incidents. Subsidiary of MRI Software.

Leaseweb.history: Founded 1997 in the Netherlands by Con Zwinkels and Laurens Rosenthal. One of Europe's largest hosting providers. Subsidiary of OCOM. Operates globally serving over 20,000 customers. Known historically for hosting controversial content; faced scrutiny over cybercrime hosting in its early years.
Leaseweb.summary_abuse: Leaseweb has a documented history of hosting bulletproof or abuse-tolerant content, though it has improved enforcement over the years. As a large hosting provider with global infrastructure, it has been used by botnets, phishing operations, and malware distribution networks. The company has SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA certifications. Abuse reports can be filed but historical response times have been criticised.

Lede.history: Lede is a publishing CMS platform and growth program designed specifically for journalism startups and news media organizations. No notable history events documented.
Lede.summary_abuse: Lede is a journalism-focused CMS with no notable abuse signal. It serves news media organizations and has no consumer-facing surface typically exploited by scammers.

Legal Monster.history: Founded 2018 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Søren Stenderup Jensen, Wendell Brown, and Leif Bode Nielsen. Specialises in GDPR and ePrivacy consent management. The company later rebranded to Openli. No notable acquisitions recorded.
Legal Monster.summary_abuse: Minimal abuse risk. Legal Monster is a consent and privacy management platform for GDPR/ePrivacy compliance. Its purpose-built privacy tooling reduces rather than enables abuse. No known misuse incidents or security incidents documented.

Lemon Squeezy.history: Founded 2020 in Salt Lake City, Utah by Gilbert Pellegrom and J.R. Farr. Built as a Merchant of Record payment platform for SaaS companies. Acquired by Stripe in July 2024 for an undisclosed amount. Now operates as a Stripe subsidiary.
Lemon Squeezy.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse potential given it is a payment processing and merchant-of-record platform. Used by software developers and digital product sellers. As a Stripe subsidiary, benefits from Stripe's fraud detection and compliance infrastructure. PCI-DSS compliant. Potential for misuse in selling counterfeit digital goods, though Stripe's AUP applies.

Lendi.history: Lendi is a Polish fintech company founded circa 2010, headquartered in Szczecin, Poland. Provides a loan comparison and brokerage platform for mortgages and consumer loans, with over 1,650 financial experts. Operates primarily in the Polish market. No notable acquisitions or funding rounds on record.
Lendi.summary_abuse: Low to moderate abuse risk for a financial comparison platform. Primarily a loan brokerage serving Polish consumers. No notable security incidents or fraudulent activity documented. GDPR compliant as a Polish EU-based business.

Lengow.history: Founded July 2009 in Nantes, France by Mickael Froger and Jeremie Peiro. Expanded internationally from 2012 with teams in Paris and other markets. Received majority growth investment from Marlin Equity Partners in February 2021. Serves over 3,600 e-commerce customers globally.
Lengow.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B SaaS for e-commerce product feed management and channel automation. No consumer-facing surface. GDPR compliant as a French EU company. No known misuse incidents.

Lenis.history: Created by Studio Freight (now darkroom.engineering) around 2021. Open-source JavaScript smooth-scroll library. Became popular in the creative web agency community for WebGL and parallax integrations. Package was renamed from @studio-freight/lenis to lenis.
Lenis.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Frontend JavaScript library with no server-side component or data collection.

Lenus.history: Founded 2016 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Bertram Thorslund. Provides a platform for online fitness coaches to manage clients and scale coaching businesses. Raised over €26M including a record-breaking Danish Series A from EQT Ventures, EIFO, and others. Expanded to offices in 9+ countries by 2022.
Lenus.summary_abuse: Low abuse risk. Health coaching SaaS enabling fitness coaches to manage clients. No known data misuse or fraudulent activity. GDPR compliant as a Danish EU company. No consumer-facing anonymous features that could be exploited.

LEPTON.history: LEPTON is a PHP-based open-source CMS that emerged as a fork of WebsiteBaker around 2007, aiming for a leaner, modular architecture. It targets technically-minded webmasters wanting a lightweight alternative to larger CMSs like Joomla or WordPress. The project has a small but committed European user community and is maintained by volunteers.
LEPTON.summary_abuse: LEPTON is a self-hosted open-source CMS with no known notable abuse patterns. As a niche CMS with a small user base, it has minimal attack surface exposure compared to mainstream platforms. The self-hosted model means no centralized abuse concern. No documented security incidents or abuse campaigns targeting LEPTON specifically.

Leptos.history: Leptos is an open-source full-stack Rust web framework created by Greg Johnston around 2022. It uses fine-grained reactivity inspired by Solid.js and compiles to WebAssembly. Active community and rapid development with growing adoption in the Rust ecosystem.
Leptos.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Open-source Rust web framework with no server-side data collection or consumer-facing features.

Less.history: Less.js was created by Alexis Sellier in 2009 as a CSS preprocessor that extends CSS with variables, mixins, and functions. Originally written in Ruby, then ported to JavaScript. Bootstrap v3 was built with Less, greatly boosting adoption. Has since lost ground to Sass/SCSS but remains widely used.
Less.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Client-side CSS preprocessor with no data collection or server component.

Let's Connect.history: Let's Connect is an Austrian digital communication and customer interaction management platform. No notable history events documented.
Let's Connect.summary_abuse: Let's Connect is a small Austrian live chat and customer communications SaaS with no notable abuse signal.

Let's Encrypt.history: Founded 2014 by the Internet Security Research Group (ISRG) and officially launched in April 2016. Backed by Mozilla, Cisco, Akamai, EFF, and others. Let's Encrypt provides free, automated TLS certificates and has issued over 3 billion certificates. It revolutionised HTTPS adoption across the web. Operated as a nonprofit under ISRG.
Let's Encrypt.summary_abuse: Let's Encrypt certificates are free and widely available, which means phishing and malware sites frequently use Let's Encrypt certificates to appear legitimate. Research has documented Let's Encrypt certificates on a meaningful share of phishing sites. However the organisation has no way to prevent this as certificate issuance is automated for any domain-validated request; it is not a hosting provider and has no content moderation role. Abuse is inherent to the open CA model, not a vendor failure.

Letro.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Letro.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Levar.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Levar.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Level 5.history: Level 5 is a managed WordPress page builder product offered through WP Engine, combining WordPress with WP Engine managed hosting and caching optimisations. No dedicated company history as a standalone entity.
Level 5.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Managed WordPress page builder tied to WP Engine hosting. WP Engine has SOC 2 certification and GDPR compliance. No known specific abuse incidents associated with Level 5 brand.

Level Access.history: Founded 1999 in Arlington, Virginia by Tim Springer and Mark Steele, specialising in digital accessibility compliance (ADA, WCAG, Section 508). Raised $111M total including a KKR-led Series C in 2021. Merged with eSSENTIAL Accessibility in 2022 to form an expanded accessibility solutions provider with 501-1000 employees.
Level Access.summary_abuse: Minimal abuse potential. Enterprise digital accessibility compliance and auditing platform. No consumer-facing surface. No known misuse incidents. Privacy-respecting by design given its mission.

Lever.history: Founded 2012 in San Francisco by Nate Smith, Sarah Nahm, Randal Truong, and Brian Noguchi. Raised $123M total. Acquired by Employ Inc. (backed by K1 Investment Management) in August 2022, joining Jobvite and JazzHR in the Employ portfolio.
Lever.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B ATS platform used by HR teams. Processes employee candidate data. No known data breaches or misuse incidents of note. SOC 2 certified and GDPR compliant.

LEVERADE.history: LEVERADE is a Spanish sports management and monetization platform founded in 2012. It offers tools for sports federations, clubs, and event organizers to manage competitions, registrations, and financial operations. The company is based in Spain and has focused on the European sports management market, particularly for smaller federations and clubs that need affordable software.
LEVERADE.summary_abuse: LEVERADE is a niche sports management SaaS platform serving sports organizations. As a B2B platform primarily used by federations and clubs, it has a limited public-facing abuse surface. No notable security incidents or abuse patterns documented. Standard B2B SaaS data handling risks apply.

Lexer.history: Lexer is an Australian customer data and AI platform (CDP) founded circa 2012, headquartered in Melbourne. Serves retail and consumer brands globally. Raised venture funding from investors including Blackbird Ventures. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes on record.
Lexer.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B customer data platform for retail analytics. Processes first-party customer data on behalf of retail clients. SOC 2 certified. No known misuse incidents.

Lexity.history: Founded 2009 (originally as Vurve) by Amit Kumar. An e-commerce analytics and app platform for SMB merchants. Raised over $5.5M before being acquired by Yahoo in July 2013 as Yahoo's 19th acquisition under CEO Marissa Mayer. Service was subsequently shut down after the acquisition.
Lexity.summary_abuse: Low abuse signal. Defunct e-commerce analytics tool acquired by Yahoo and shut down. No notable security or abuse incidents documented.

LGC.history: LGC is a small Italian CMS described as a modern website management system, hosted at luigigabrieleconti.com. No notable history documented beyond its creation.
LGC.summary_abuse: LGC is a very small CMS with no notable abuse signal or documented use in malicious contexts.

Liana.history: Liana is a Finnish CMS and digital marketing platform developed by Liana Technologies, founded in 2003 in Helsinki. Focuses on website and e-commerce solutions primarily for Nordic and European markets. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes.
Liana.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B CMS for Finnish/European enterprise websites. GDPR compliant as a Finnish company. No known misuse incidents.

Liberapay.history: Liberapay is an open-source, non-profit recurring donation platform founded in 2015 in France. Created as a free alternative to Patreon and Gratipay. Operated by a non-profit association. Source code is MIT-licensed and publicly available on GitHub.
Liberapay.summary_abuse: Low to moderate abuse potential. Open-source recurring payment platform used to fund creators and open-source developers. Free with no KYC requirements for small transactions, which could be exploited for money laundering or fraudulent fundraising. Non-profit operator with limited enforcement capacity. No major documented abuse incidents.

libphonenumber.history: libphonenumber is a JavaScript port of Google's libphonenumber library for parsing, formatting, and validating international phone numbers. The original Java library was developed by Google engineers and open-sourced around 2009; JavaScript ports followed and are widely used.
libphonenumber.summary_abuse: libphonenumber has no notable abuse signal; it is a phone number validation library with no server-side component or consumer-facing surface.

Libravatar.history: Libravatar is an open-source federated avatar hosting service, founded around 2011 as a privacy-respecting alternative to Gravatar. AGPLv3-licensed, self-hostable. Maintained by a small community with limited activity in recent years.
Libravatar.summary_abuse: Minimal abuse potential. Privacy-respecting federated avatar service with no notable security incidents. Niche tool used mainly by open-source communities.

libwww-perl-daemon.history: libwww-perl-daemon is the HTTP server component of the libwww-perl collection, a set of Perl modules for HTTP client/server programming. The libwww-perl project has been maintained since the early 1990s and is a foundational Perl HTTP library.
libwww-perl-daemon.summary_abuse: libwww-perl-daemon has no notable abuse signal as a Perl HTTP server library. It has no consumer-facing surface.

Lieferando.history: Lieferando is Germany's dominant food delivery portal, founded 2008 in Berlin. Acquired by Takeaway.com in 2013, which later merged with Just Eat in 2020 to form Just Eat Takeaway.com (now NYSE and Amsterdam listed). Lieferando is the primary brand for Germany within the JET group.
Lieferando.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse risk typical for large consumer-facing marketplace platforms. As Germany's dominant food delivery platform, Lieferando has been used for account takeover attacks targeting delivery couriers and customers. Heavy tracker load on the platform. Just Eat Takeaway group has documented GDPR compliance and responsible disclosure programs. Phishing campaigns impersonating Lieferando order notifications are occasionally reported.

Liferay.history: Founded 2004 by Bryan Cheung in Los Angeles. Open-source enterprise portal and digital experience platform. One of the leading Java-based enterprise portal solutions. Has grown to serve Fortune 500 companies globally with both community and enterprise editions.
Liferay.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Enterprise portal platform used by large organisations for internal intranets and public portals. SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR certified. Self-hosted deployments are common, limiting vendor-side abuse exposure. No notable misuse incidents.

Lift.history: Lift (Liftweb) is an open-source web framework for Scala, created by David Pollak in 2007. Known for its security-first design and Comet/push technology. Once popular in Scala enterprise development, its adoption has declined as newer Scala frameworks emerged.
Lift.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Server-side Scala web framework with no consumer-facing features or data collection.

Lifter Apps Pop-up Window.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Lifter Apps Pop-up Window.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

LifterLMS.history: LifterLMS is a WordPress LMS plugin founded in 2014 by Chris Badgett and Thomas Levy. Provides a free core plugin with paid add-ons for selling and managing online courses on WordPress sites. One of the most popular WordPress LMS plugins.
LifterLMS.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. WordPress plugin for building online courses. Self-hosted by site owners. No mass-email or content-hosting features that invite widespread abuse. No known security incidents.

LiftIgniter.history: LiftIgniter was founded as Petametrics Inc. as a machine-learning personalization platform for digital publishers. It was a Y Combinator graduate. In March 2020 it was acquired by Maven (The Arena Group) as part of Maven's media technology consolidation.
LiftIgniter.summary_abuse: LiftIgniter is a content personalization platform for publishers with no notable abuse signal. It operates in the B2B media technology space.

Lightbox.history: Lightbox is a JavaScript image overlay library created by Lokesh Dhakar in 2005. One of the original and most-cloned jQuery-era plugins, spawning many forks and derivatives. Still widely used on older websites for displaying images in modal overlays.
Lightbox.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Client-side JavaScript image overlay library with no data collection or server component.

Lightfolio.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Lightfolio.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

LightMon Engine.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
LightMon Engine.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Lightning.history: Lightning is a free WordPress theme developed by Vektor, Inc., a Japanese WordPress agency. GPL-licensed and actively maintained. Popular in the Japanese WordPress community.
Lightning.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Free WordPress theme with no data collection. Distributed through WordPress theme repository.

Lightspeed eCom.history: Lightspeed eCom originated from the acquisition of Amsterdam-based SEOShop by Lightspeed Commerce in October 2015. Lightspeed Commerce itself was founded by Dax da Silva in 2005 in Montreal, Canada. Lightspeed is now publicly traded on NYSE and TSX (LSPD), serving retail, hospitality, and golf businesses in 100+ countries.
Lightspeed eCom.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B e-commerce platform for retail merchants. PCI-DSS compliant for payment processing. SOC 2 certified. Publicly traded on NYSE and TSX. No notable misuse patterns documented.

LightSpeed VT.history: LightSpeed VT is a video-based LMS platform used for employee training and sales enablement. Founded in the early 2000s in the US. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes on public record.
LightSpeed VT.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Enterprise video-based LMS for corporate training. No consumer-facing surface. No documented security incidents.

lighttpd.history: Lighttpd (pronounced 'lighty') was created by Jan Kneschke in 2003 as a lightweight, high-performance web server designed for speed-critical environments. It gained significant adoption for serving static content and was notable for its event-driven architecture. While Apache and Nginx dominate the web server market, Lighttpd remains actively used in embedded and high-performance deployments.
lighttpd.summary_abuse: Lighttpd has no notable abuse signal as a general-purpose web server. Like any web server it can be deployed on abusive infrastructure, but the software itself is neutral infrastructure and its presence does not indicate malicious activity.

Ligna.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Ligna.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Liine.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Liine.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Lily.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Lily.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Lime Talk.history: Lime Talk is a Czech live chat and customer messaging SaaS for websites. Small startup based in Czech Republic. GDPR compliant as an EU company. No notable ownership changes.
Lime Talk.summary_abuse: Low to moderate abuse risk. Live chat platform embedded on websites could be used for phishing or social engineering if the website itself is malicious. No known vendor-specific abuse patterns or incidents.

Limecall.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Limecall.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

LimeChat.history: LimeChat is an Indian AI chatbot and conversational commerce platform, describing itself as India's first level-3 AI chatbot company. Founded circa 2020 in India, serving D2C e-commerce brands.
LimeChat.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. AI chatbot for D2C e-commerce. No consumer-facing anonymous features. No documented security incidents.

Limepay.history: Limepay is an Australian buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) platform integrated with merchant payment flows. Small fintech startup founded around 2018. PCI-DSS compliant for payment processing.
Limepay.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse risk inherent to BNPL platforms. BNPL services are known targets for account takeover and fraudulent purchase fraud. Limepay is PCI-DSS compliant. No major documented incidents specific to Limepay.

LimeSpot.history: LimeSpot is a Canadian AI personalisation and product recommendation platform for e-commerce, primarily serving Shopify merchants. Founded circa 2013. No notable acquisitions or ownership changes on record.
LimeSpot.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. E-commerce AI personalisation SaaS. Collects browsing behaviour data for product recommendations. No notable security incidents documented.

Limit Login Attempts Reloaded.history: Limit Login Attempts Reloaded is a WordPress plugin that blocks brute-force login attacks. A reloaded fork of the original 'Limit Login Attempts' plugin by Johan Eenfeldt (2009). The reloaded version added cloud-based lockout lists and is one of the most popular WordPress security plugins with millions of active installs.
Limit Login Attempts Reloaded.summary_abuse: Low abuse signal. Security plugin specifically designed to prevent brute-force attacks. No consumer-facing anonymous surface. The cloud lockout feature may send IP data to a third-party service. No documented misuse incidents.

Linda.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Linda.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Lindy.history: Founded 2023 in San Francisco by Florent Crivello. AI assistant platform with no-code workflow automation. Raised $50M+ from Battery Ventures and others. $5.1M ARR in 2024 with 37 employees.
Lindy.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse potential given it is an AI automation platform that can act on behalf of users. Access to email and calendars could be misused if accounts are compromised. No documented specific incidents. New platform without established security track record.

LINE Login.history: LINE Login is the OAuth-based social authentication API provided by LINE, the dominant messaging platform in Japan, Taiwan, Thailand, and Indonesia. LINE was originally developed by NHN Japan (now Naver) and launched in 2011. The parent company was reorganized as LINE Corporation and eventually merged with Yahoo Japan to form LY Corporation in 2023. LINE Login enables third-party apps and websites to authenticate users via their LINE account, with over 200 million users accessible.
LINE Login.summary_abuse: LINE Login is a social authentication provider backed by LY Corporation, a major Japanese tech company. As an auth provider, it processes identity data for users authenticating via LINE accounts. LINE has faced scrutiny in Japan and South Korea over data storage practices, with the Japanese government investigating data transfers to overseas servers in 2021. LY Corporation subsequently implemented stricter domestic data handling. No direct abuse of the LINE Login API itself documented.

Line-Up.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Line-Up.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Line2.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Line2.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Linear.history: Founded 2019 in San Francisco by Karri Saarinen, Tuomas Artman, and Jori Lallo (alumni of Airbnb, Coinbase, Uber). Issue tracking and project management tool known for exceptional performance and design. Raised $134M from Accel and Sequoia Capital. Reached unicorn status (>$1B valuation) in 2025.
Linear.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B issue tracking and project management SaaS for software teams. SOC 2 certified, GDPR compliant. No known misuse incidents.

Linen.history: Linen is an open-source real-time community chat platform built as an alternative to Slack for open communities. Founded circa 2021 in the US. Apache 2.0 licensed with self-hosting option. Raised seed funding from YC and others.
Linen.summary_abuse: Low to moderate abuse potential. Community chat platform with public channels could host spam or harassment if moderation is weak. Open-source and self-hostable, so vendor abuse controls are limited to the hosted version.

Linguana.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Linguana.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Linguise.history: Linguise is a French automatic website translation SaaS. Founded circa 2021. Provides front-end translation with AI/machine translation and a translation editor. GDPR compliant as an EU company.
Linguise.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Automatic website translation service. GDPR compliant. No consumer-facing anonymous features. No documented security incidents.

Linkedin Ads.history: LinkedIn was founded 2003 by Reid Hoffman and others. Acquired by Microsoft in 2016 for $26.2B. LinkedIn Ads (Campaign Manager) is LinkedIn's B2B advertising platform, allowing targeting by job title, industry, company size, and more.
Linkedin Ads.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse risk for a major advertising platform. LinkedIn Ads can be used for social engineering and spear-phishing campaigns targeting professionals. Account takeovers allow fraudulent ad spending. LinkedIn has significant fraud detection capabilities given Microsoft backing, but ad targeting features can be weaponised for social engineering. No endemic takedown issues.

Linkedin Insight Tag.history: The LinkedIn Insight Tag is a lightweight JavaScript tracking pixel offered by LinkedIn, a platform founded in 2003 and acquired by Microsoft in 2016. It enables conversion tracking and website audience building for LinkedIn Ads. Requires explicit consent under GDPR.
Linkedin Insight Tag.summary_abuse: Significant privacy concern. The Insight Tag tracks website visitors across the web regardless of whether they interact with LinkedIn Ads. It enables cross-site tracking of LinkedIn members. Under GDPR, explicit consent is required. The tag has been cited in multiple privacy investigations. LinkedIn (Microsoft) has SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications and maintains a bug bounty programme.

Linkedin Sign-in.history: LinkedIn Sign-In is the OAuth-based social login feature of LinkedIn, a platform founded in 2003 and acquired by Microsoft in 2016 for $26.2B. It allows users to log into third-party applications using their LinkedIn credentials.
Linkedin Sign-in.summary_abuse: Moderate privacy risk. LinkedIn Sign-In exposes professional profile data to third-party applications. Has been used in phishing and account harvesting campaigns. LinkedIn (Microsoft) has strong security infrastructure, bug bounty, and SOC 2/ISO 27001 compliance. Abuse primarily targets users via rogue OAuth apps requesting excessive permissions.

Linkfire.history: Linkfire is a Danish music marketing smart-links platform founded 2014 in Copenhagen. Used by major labels including Universal, Sony, and Warner. Provides a single landing page linking to all streaming platforms. Raised venture funding and serves thousands of music artists globally.
Linkfire.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse risk for a URL redirect and link aggregation service. Smart links could be used for phishing redirects targeting music fans. Link shortening/redirect features are commonly abused to hide malicious destinations. Legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates. GDPR compliant as Danish company. No major documented abuse incidents specific to Linkfire.

LinkMink.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
LinkMink.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Linkz.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Linkz.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Linx Commerce.history: Linx Commerce is an e-commerce platform developed by Linx, a Brazilian retail technology company. Linx was founded in 2001 in São Paulo and was acquired by TOTVS (a major Brazilian ERP company) in 2021. Linx Commerce serves Brazilian retail enterprises.
Linx Commerce.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. B2B e-commerce platform for Brazilian retail enterprises. PCI-DSS compliant for payment processing. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Linx Impulse.history: Linx Impulse is a personalisation, media retargeting, and recommendation engine for Brazilian retail, developed by Linx (founded 2001 in São Paulo, acquired by TOTVS in 2021). Tracks user behaviour for targeted ad retargeting across retail e-commerce.
Linx Impulse.summary_abuse: Moderate privacy concern. Linx Impulse collects browsing and purchase behaviour for retargeting, placing it in ad-tech adjacent territory. Heavy tracker density on integrated sites. No GDPR applicability as primarily a Brazilian product. No documented specific abuse incidents.

LipScore.history: LipScore is a Norwegian ratings and reviews SaaS for e-commerce, founded circa 2014. GDPR compliant as a Norwegian EU EEA company. No notable ownership changes.
LipScore.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Ratings and reviews platform. Potential for fake review submission but vendor-side protections against this are standard. GDPR compliant. No documented security incidents.

Liquid Web.history: Liquid Web was founded 1997 in Lansing, Michigan. Premium managed web hosting provider focused on WordPress and WooCommerce hosting. Backed by Madison Dearborn Partners (PE) since 2015. Known for 'heroic support'. Serves SMB and enterprise hosting customers.
Liquid Web.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse risk typical for web hosting providers. As a major managed hosting provider, Liquid Web hosts customer sites that may include spam or phishing pages. SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA compliant. Has AUP and abuse reporting process. No documented major hosting abuse incidents of note.

Liscio.history: Liscio is a US client communication and document management SaaS for accounting firms and CPAs. Founded circa 2016. HIPAA and SOC 2 compliant given its handling of sensitive financial and personal data in accounting workflows.
Liscio.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Accounting firm client portal and document exchange SaaS. Handles sensitive financial data with SOC 2 and HIPAA compliance. No notable security incidents documented.

List.js.history: List.js is a lightweight JavaScript library for adding search, sort, and filter to lists or tables in HTML, created by Jonny Strömberg in 2012. MIT-licensed. Widely used in static HTML pages and simple web apps.
