List.js.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Client-side JavaScript utility library with no data collection or server component.

Listagram.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Listagram.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Listrak.history: Listrak was founded in 2000 in Lititz, Pennsylvania. Marketing automation and CDP platform for retail, specialising in email, SMS, and cross-channel marketing. One of the longer-running retail email platforms in the US market.
Listrak.summary_abuse: Moderate-high abuse risk typical for bulk email marketing platforms. Listrak enables mass email and SMS marketing; like all ESP/MA platforms it can be misused for spam campaigns. SOC 2 certified and has an AUP. The retail focus (opted-in subscriber lists) keeps abuse below the worst offenders, but bulk email remains a spam vector.

ListTrac.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
ListTrac.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

lit-element.history: lit-element is a lightweight base class for creating web components developed by Google Polymer team, first released in 2018. It evolved from the earlier Polymer project and was later merged into the Lit library (combining lit-element and lit-html) in 2021.
lit-element.summary_abuse: lit-element has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side web component library with no server-side component or consumer-facing surface.

lit-html.history: lit-html is a JavaScript HTML templating library developed by the Google Polymer team, first released around 2017. It was later merged with lit-element into the unified Lit library in 2021.
lit-html.summary_abuse: lit-html has no notable abuse signal; it is a client-side HTML templating library with no server-side component.

lite-youtube-embed.history: lite-youtube-embed is an open-source JavaScript library created by Paul Irish that renders lightweight YouTube video thumbnails and only loads the full YouTube iframe when the user clicks to play, improving page performance. No notable ownership changes.
lite-youtube-embed.summary_abuse: lite-youtube-embed has no notable abuse signal; it is a performance-optimization library for YouTube embeds with no server-side component.

LiteSpeed.history: LiteSpeed Web Server was developed by LiteSpeed Technologies, founded in 2002 in the US. A high-performance Apache drop-in replacement that became popular for PHP hosting environments. Developed OpenLiteSpeed (free) and the commercial LiteSpeed Web Server. Widely adopted by shared hosting providers.
LiteSpeed.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Server-side web server software installed by hosting administrators. No data collection or consumer-facing surface. No notable security incidents documented.

LiteSpeed Cache.history: LiteSpeed Cache is a free WordPress caching and optimisation plugin developed by LiteSpeed Technologies. Released around 2014, it became one of the most popular WordPress plugins with millions of active installs. GPL-3.0 licensed. Requires a LiteSpeed web server for full functionality.
LiteSpeed Cache.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Server-side WordPress caching plugin. No data collection from site visitors. No notable security incidents documented.

Litespeed Cache.history: LiteSpeed Cache is a free WordPress caching and optimisation plugin developed by LiteSpeed Technologies. Released around 2014, it became one of the most popular WordPress plugins with millions of active installs. GPL-3.0 licensed. Requires a LiteSpeed web server for full functionality.
Litespeed Cache.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Server-side WordPress caching plugin. No data collection from site visitors. No notable security incidents documented.

Lithium.history: Lithium Technologies was founded 2001 and built one of the leading online community and social media management platforms. Acquired by Vista Equity Partners in 2017. Merged with Spredfast in October 2018 to form Khoros. Khoros was subsequently acquired by IgniteTech in May 2025. The Lithium brand name persists as a legacy identifier.
Lithium.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Enterprise community and social engagement platform used by large organisations for branded communities. SOC 2 certified and GDPR compliant. No notable security or abuse incidents.

Litium.history: Litium is a Swedish e-commerce and digital commerce platform, founded circa 2004 in Stockholm. Primarily serves Nordic retail and B2B companies. PCI-DSS compliant and GDPR compliant as a Swedish EU company.
Litium.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Enterprise e-commerce platform for Nordic retail businesses. GDPR compliant. PCI-DSS certified for payment handling. No notable security incidents documented.

Litmus.history: Litmus was founded 2005 in Boston, Massachusetts. Email testing, analytics, and previewing SaaS used by email marketers and designers. Raised over $50M in VC funding. Acquired Stensul in 2023. One of the leading email marketing platform tools globally.
Litmus.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Email testing and analytics platform for legitimate marketing teams. Not a bulk email sending service. SOC 2 certified and GDPR compliant. No notable security or abuse incidents.

Little Hotelier.history: Little Hotelier is a property management system for small accommodation providers (B&Bs, guesthouses), developed by SiteMinder, an Australian hospitality technology company founded in 2006 and listed on ASX. SiteMinder is one of the leading hotel technology platforms globally.
Little Hotelier.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Hospitality PMS for small accommodation properties. PCI-DSS compliant for payment processing. No notable security incidents documented.

Littledata.history: Littledata is a UK-based e-commerce analytics SaaS founded in 2016, providing accurate tracking and data pipelines for Shopify stores, connecting to Google Analytics, Segment, and other platforms. GDPR compliant as a UK company.
Littledata.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Shopify analytics and data pipeline tool. GDPR compliant. No notable security or abuse incidents documented.

Live Story.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Live Story.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

LiveAgent.history: LiveAgent was developed by Quality Unit, a Slovak software company founded in 2004 in Bratislava. Launched as a live chat and help desk platform in 2011. One of the top-rated help desk software platforms. GDPR compliant as an EU company. On-premise self-hosted option available.
LiveAgent.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Help desk and live chat SaaS from a Slovak EU company. GDPR compliant. PCI-DSS certified. No notable security incidents documented.

LiveBooks.history: LiveBooks (liveBooks) was founded in 2003 by Michael Costuros, Tibi Craciun, Andy Patrick, and Matt Bailey — photographers themselves — to help creative professionals showcase their work online. Headquartered in Austin, Texas, the company offers customizable website templates specifically designed for photographers, videographers, and artists. No notable ownership changes documented.
LiveBooks.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveBooks is a niche website builder for photographers and creatives with a paid subscription model. Limited free tier reduces anonymous abuse risk. No documented notable abuse incidents. Typical minor risk from content hosting on shared domain, but platforms of this size maintain basic abuse controls.

LiveBy.history: LiveBy was founded in 2015 by Cory Scott and Matan Gill in Lincoln, Nebraska, as a hyperlocal neighborhood data and community insights platform for real estate professionals. The company was acquired by Fathom Holdings in 2021, and later acquired by moveconcierge.com in December 2025. As of 2026, rebranded as LiveBy Local launching automated hyperlocal marketing tools for real estate agents.
LiveBy.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveBy is a B2B real estate data platform serving licensed real estate agents with neighborhood and community data. Narrow professional audience with low abuse surface. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveCanvas.history: LiveCanvas is a Bootstrap-based WordPress page builder plugin designed for web developers who want direct HTML/CSS control. Built on Bootstrap 5, it targets developers who prefer coding over visual drag-and-drop builders. Emerged around 2019. One-time license at $99 with lifetime updates. Solo or very small independent developer project with no notable ownership changes.
LiveCanvas.summary_abuse: Very low abuse potential. LiveCanvas is a self-hosted WordPress plugin sold on a one-time license basis. No hosted content surface and no anonymous sign-up — customers install it on their own WordPress site. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveChat.history: LiveChat was founded in 2002 by Mariusz Cieply in Wroclaw, Poland. It launched as one of the early commercial live chat platforms and grew into a publicly-listed company (Warsaw Stock Exchange). In 2014 they rebranded from LiveChat Software to Text (company) as parent entity, now operating multiple products including LiveChat, ChatBot, HelpDesk, and KnowledgeBase under the Text group. Revenue reached $80.6M in 2024.
LiveChat.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveChat is a legitimate, publicly-traded B2B SaaS chat platform from Poland with 33,000 customers. GDPR-compliant EU company. No documented notable abuse incidents. Subscription model with identity verification reduces anonymous abuse risk.

LiveEdit.history: LiveEdit Platform is a cloud-based CMS and website builder primarily targeting businesses in the health, fitness, wellness, and beauty space. It integrates tightly with MINDBODY software for scheduling and management. Used by multi-location business franchises. No widely documented founding date; platform appears to have been operating since the 2000s under LiveEdit Aurora brand.
LiveEdit.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveEdit is a niche B2B website builder for fitness and wellness businesses with MINDBODY integration. Subscription model, primarily used by legitimate brick-and-mortar businesses. No documented abuse incidents.

Livefyre.history: Livefyre was founded in December 2009 by Jordan Kretchmer and Henry Arlander as a social commenting and engagement platform. Adobe and Salesforce Ventures led a $47M investment in 2015. Adobe acquired Livefyre in May 2016 and integrated it into Adobe Experience Manager. The service was officially shut down by Adobe in 2021.
Livefyre.summary_abuse: Deprecated service shut down in 2021. No current abuse potential. Was a social commenting platform used by major media organizations before Adobe acquisition and shutdown. No documented abuse incidents during its operation.

LiveHelp.history: LiveHelp is a live chat and digital customer care platform developed by Sostanza srl, an Italian software company based in Gorle, Lombardia. Founded in 2004 with 20 years of experience in web services. Positioned as an omnichannel customer service suite for Italian and European businesses. No notable ownership changes documented.
LiveHelp.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveHelp is a niche Italian B2B live chat SaaS serving European businesses. GDPR-compliant EU company. Subscription model with no free anonymous tier. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveIntent.history: LiveIntent was founded in 2009 by Matthew Keiser in New York City as a people-based email advertising and marketing platform. Raised Series C funding of $20M in 2015. In October 2024, LiveIntent was acquired by Zeta Global, a publicly-traded data-driven marketing cloud company.
LiveIntent.summary_abuse: Moderate abuse potential as an ad-tech platform that enables audience targeting via email. LiveIntent tracks users across websites and email newsletters for ad targeting, typical of the email advertising ecosystem. Privacy concerns include cross-site tracking via email open pixels and identity resolution. No specific public incidents, but the platform is ad-tech adjacent by nature.

Liveinternet.history: LiveInternet (liveinternet.ru) is a Russian web portal and analytics service launched around 2001, originally operating as a website rating counter (rax.ru) and online blog/diary community (li.ru). It grew to become a significant web analytics provider for Russian-language websites and a popular blogging platform within the Cyrillic web. Based in Moscow, Russia.
Liveinternet.summary_abuse: Moderate privacy and trust concerns. Liveinternet is a Russian web analytics tracker embedded on many websites, collecting visitor data under Russian jurisdiction with no GDPR compliance. Free analytics counter embeds allow broad data collection across the Russian-language web. No documented direct fraud or phishing use, but data sovereignty concerns are significant for non-Russian users.

LiveJournal.history: LiveJournal was created by Brad Fitzpatrick in 1999 as a personal blogging tool. It was acquired by Six Apart in 2005, then sold to Russian media company SUP Media in 2007. In December 2016, all servers were relocated to Russia, and in April 2017, the terms of service were updated to conform to Russian law, requiring compliance with Russian content regulations. This raised major user concerns about censorship and data privacy, triggering a mass exodus of users from Russia and internationally. Ownership later passed to Rambler&Co.
LiveJournal.summary_abuse: Elevated abuse and privacy concerns. LiveJournal operates under Russian law after server relocation in 2016, with terms of service compliant with Russian censorship requirements. GDPR non-compliant due to Russian data storage. Free tier with open user content hosting creates some risk of spam and abuse. Historical significance in early blogging but significantly diminished in Western users.

LivePerson.history: LivePerson was founded in 1995 by Robert LoCascio in New York City and went public on NASDAQ in April 2000. Known for developing the Conversational Cloud platform enabling consumer-to-brand messaging. Pivoted toward AI-driven conversational commerce in 2018. In 2025-2026, SoundHound AI announced acquisition of LivePerson for approximately $43M in an all-stock deal expected to close in H2 2026.
LivePerson.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LivePerson is a publicly-traded enterprise conversational AI platform with major brand customers. Enterprise sales-only model with no anonymous sign-up. SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA compliant. No notable abuse incidents documented.

LiveRamp DPM.history: LiveRamp DPM (Data Plus Math) refers to LiveRamp's TV advertising measurement capabilities, derived from the acquisition of Data Plus Math in 2019 for $150M. Data Plus Math was founded in 2014 by John Hoctor and Anthony Schiffino to provide identity-based cross-screen TV measurement. LiveRamp Holdings itself was incorporated as a spinout in 2011 and became an independent public company in 2018 after separating from Acxiom.
LiveRamp DPM.summary_abuse: Low-moderate abuse potential as an ad-tech data connectivity platform. LiveRamp DPM (Data Plus Math) is a TV measurement and identity-resolution service used by major broadcast and streaming companies. Enterprise-only sales model limits direct abuse surface. The underlying data practices involve identity graph construction and cross-device tracking, raising privacy concerns but operated within industry standards. No documented direct abuse incidents.

LiveRamp PCM.history: LiveRamp PCM (Preference and Consent Management) is a privacy compliance module within the LiveRamp platform, designed to help organizations comply with GDPR, CCPA, ePrivacy Directive, and other data protection regulations. It is part of LiveRamp Holdings, which became an independent public company in 2018 after separating from Acxiom. LiveRamp was originally founded in 2011.
LiveRamp PCM.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveRamp PCM is a consent and preference management platform designed specifically to help enterprises comply with GDPR, CCPA, and similar privacy laws. It is an enterprise-only product with no public sign-up. Its core purpose is privacy compliance, making it an inherently low-abuse-risk tool. No documented abuse incidents.

Livescale.history: Livescale was founded in 2016 by Virgile Ollivier and Laurent Boutet in Montreal, Canada, as a live shopping and social commerce platform. The company enables consumer brands to host interactive live shopping events with integrated checkout. Notable customer L'Oreal launched with them in 2019. Raised $2.5M seed funding. Partners with Shopify, Worldline, and Renowned for expanded e-commerce capabilities.
Livescale.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. Livescale is a Canadian B2B live shopping platform for consumer brands. Subscription-based with no free public sign-up. Primarily used by legitimate fashion, beauty, and consumer goods brands. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveSession.history: LiveSession was founded by Kamil Drazkiewicz as a session recording and product analytics platform based in Poland. Launched around 2018, it competes with tools like Hotjar and FullStory. Offers session replays, event analytics, and conversion funnels. GDPR-compliant as a Polish/EU company. No major ownership changes documented.
LiveSession.summary_abuse: Low-moderate abuse potential. LiveSession records user sessions on client websites, raising some privacy considerations for website visitors whose behavior is captured. Data is processed by LiveSession as a processor. GDPR-compliant with data processing agreements available. Free tier is limited to 1,000 sessions. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveSite.history: liveSite is a US-based open-source ASP.NET CMS and website platform that has been building custom website solutions for over 25 years. It combines CMS, e-commerce, member portals, event calendaring, blogging, and email campaigns in one platform. Originally designed for nonprofits and community organizations. Offers self-hosted community edition download.
LiveSite.summary_abuse: Very low abuse potential. liveSite is a niche self-hosted ASP.NET CMS primarily used by nonprofits and community organizations. Open-source community edition means no vendor-controlled hosting surface. No documented abuse incidents.

LiveStreet CMS.history: LiveStreet CMS is an open-source PHP/MySQL blogging and social community platform created around 2009 in Russia. It uses GPL-2.0 license and was designed for creating community websites similar to Habr (Habrahabr). The project is hosted on GitHub under the LS-SOFT organization. Development activity has significantly slowed in recent years, making it effectively dormant.
LiveStreet CMS.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential as a self-hosted open-source CMS. LiveStreet CMS users run their own installations, so the vendor has no control over hosted content. The project itself is dormant/low-activity. Niche use primarily within Russian-speaking communities. No documented vendor-level abuse incidents.

LiveTex.history: LiveTex was founded around 2010 in Saint Petersburg, Russia, as a live chat and omnichannel customer engagement platform. In 2014, the company received $4 million in investment from Vaizra Capital fund. It provides live chat, lead generation, online monitoring, and messaging channel integrations for Russian-market businesses.
LiveTex.summary_abuse: Low-moderate abuse potential. LiveTex is a Russian live chat SaaS with data stored in Russia and no GDPR compliance. Primarily serves Russian-language markets. Free trial tier could be used for minor abuse, though the platform is primarily B2B. No documented specific abuse incidents, but Russian jurisdiction reduces Western trust scores.

Livewire.history: Created by Caleb Porzio in 2019 as a full-stack framework for Laravel enabling dynamic UI without writing JavaScript. Quickly became the dominant reactive component solution for Laravel developers. Version 3 shipped in 2023 with a major rewrite; v4 followed in 2025. Officially part of the Laravel ecosystem.
Livewire.summary_abuse: Livewire is a server-side framework with no inherent consumer-facing abuse surface. It runs PHP-side on the developer's own server and does not host user content or provide free-tier infrastructure that bad actors could exploit. No notable abuse incidents documented.

LiveZilla.history: LiveZilla is a PHP/MySQL-based live chat and customer support software from Germany that offers both self-hosted and cloud deployment options. Founded around 2005, it has been used by 15,000+ users including Fortune 500 companies. Freemium model with a free tier. GDPR-compliant as a German/EU company.
LiveZilla.summary_abuse: Low abuse potential. LiveZilla is primarily a self-hosted live chat product sold as a freemium solution. Self-hosted nature limits vendor-side abuse risk. German company with GDPR compliance. No documented notable abuse incidents.

LKQD.history: LKQD was a programmatic video advertising platform founded around 2013, offering publishers tools to serve video ads across multiple devices. Nexstar Media Group acquired LKQD in 2017 to build its programmatic advertising capabilities. Verve Group subsequently acquired LKQD from Nexstar in 2021, integrating it into their global digital advertising platform. The brand has been largely absorbed into the Verve Group product portfolio.
LKQD.summary_abuse: LKQD is a programmatic video advertising platform that facilitates large-scale programmatic ad auctions involving many third-party buyers and sellers. Programmatic ad platforms are known to carry brand safety and ad fraud risks, with malvertising campaigns occasionally propagating through programmatic channels. Verve Group operates in a highly competitive ad-tech space where ad quality controls are critical. No specific public LKQD-branded abuse incidents documented post-acquisition.

LlamaLink Cloud Server.history: LlamaLink Cloud Server is a web server product from LlamaLink, a Canadian cloud solutions company founded in 2016 and headquartered in Vancouver. The server is built on Nginx and designed for load balancing and caching across hosting nodes.
LlamaLink Cloud Server.summary_abuse: LlamaLink Cloud Server is a niche hosting infrastructure product with minimal documented abuse surface. As a small Canadian cloud hosting provider, it lacks the scale or profile associated with notable abuse incidents.

Loadable-Components.history: Created by Greg Bergé in 2017 as the recommended code-splitting library for React, particularly for server-side rendering where React.lazy is insufficient. The React team has endorsed @loadable/component as the preferred third-party SSR code-splitting solution.
Loadable-Components.summary_abuse: Loadable Components is a pure client-side React library with no server component, no hosting surface, and no user data collection. No abuse risk documented.

Loadify.history: Loadify is a Shopify performance app that adds preloaders, lazy loading for images, and page transition effects to stores. It operates as a small SaaS product primarily serving the Shopify ecosystem.
Loadify.summary_abuse: Loadify is a Shopify page-speed app with no significant abuse surface. It runs embedded in merchant storefronts and collects minimal data. No notable abuse incidents documented.

LobbyPMS.history: LobbyPMS is a Brazilian hotel property management system serving over 1,400 properties across Latin America. Provides integrated PMS, channel manager, booking engine and POS. No notable ownership changes.
LobbyPMS.summary_abuse: LobbyPMS is a hospitality management SaaS with limited public-facing consumer surface. It manages internal hotel operations and reservations rather than hosting public content. No documented abuse incidents.

LocalExpress.history: LocalExpress is an AI-powered unified commerce platform for food retailers and independent grocers, providing e-commerce, mobile apps, scan-pay-and-go, and omnichannel capabilities. Founded around 2016, it targets the grocery vertical.
LocalExpress.summary_abuse: LocalExpress is a B2B grocery e-commerce platform operating within a narrow vertical. It does not host public user-generated content or provide anonymous signup. No documented abuse incidents.

LocalFocus.history: Founded in 2013 by Jelle Kamsma and two co-founders in The Hague, Netherlands. LocalFocus provides data journalism tools to regional and national newspapers, automating collection and visualization of open government data to make data-driven stories accessible to journalists.
LocalFocus.summary_abuse: LocalFocus is a B2B journalism data platform serving media organizations. It does not host public user-generated content. No documented abuse risk.

LocalGov Drupal.history: Started around 2019 when Brighton and Hove City Council shared their Drupal codebase with Croydon Council to meet new UK accessibility requirements. The collaborative open-source project grew into LocalGov Drupal, now adopted by over 63 live council websites across the UK and Ireland.
LocalGov Drupal.summary_abuse: LocalGov Drupal is a community CMS for UK local government. It is used exclusively for public sector websites and has no consumer-facing abuse risk. No documented abuse incidents.

LocaliQ Live Chat.history: LocaliQ is the digital marketing solutions arm of Gannett Co., Inc., the US media company behind USA Today. Launched as a rebrand and expansion of Gannett's digital marketing services around 2019. Live chat is part of its broader Dash AI-powered marketing platform.
LocaliQ Live Chat.summary_abuse: LocaliQ Live Chat is a business-facing chat widget managed by a large media company (Gannett). It does not facilitate user-generated public content or anonymous sign-up. Negligible abuse surface; no notable incidents documented.

Localised.history: Localised is a small analytics and personalisation SaaS platform primarily serving e-commerce businesses. Limited public information available on founding date or ownership changes.
Localised.summary_abuse: Localised is a niche e-commerce analytics tool with limited documented footprint. No significant consumer-facing abuse surface or documented incidents.

LocalSignal.history: Niche commercial SaaS with no notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
LocalSignal.summary_abuse: No notable abuse signal documented for this niche commercial SaaS.

Locksmith.history: Locksmith is a Shopify access-control app that allows merchants to restrict content by customer tag, passcode, or other conditions. It is maintained by Isaac Bowen as an independent developer product and has operated for over a decade within the Shopify ecosystem.
Locksmith.summary_abuse: Locksmith controls access to Shopify storefront pages and content. It does not host user-generated content externally. Low abuse surface; no documented incidents.

Locomotive Scroll.history: Created by Locomotive, a Montreal-based digital agency, as an open-source JavaScript library for smooth scrolling and parallax effects. Version 5 (2023) was a complete rewrite built on top of the Lenis scroll library.
Locomotive Scroll.summary_abuse: Locomotive Scroll is a client-side JavaScript animation library with no server component, no user data collection, and no consumer surface. No abuse risk.

LocomotiveCMS.history: LocomotiveCMS is a Ruby on Rails CMS created by the French agency NoCoffee around 2010, built on MongoDB and Rails. Current version 4.x was released in 2024. It targets Ruby developers seeking a flexible headless-capable CMS.
LocomotiveCMS.summary_abuse: LocomotiveCMS is a self-hosted Ruby CMS run on developers' own servers. No hosted consumer-facing component and no significant abuse surface documented.

Lodash.history: Created by John-David Dalton in 2012 as a fork of Underscore.js, offering improved performance and consistent cross-environment behavior. By 2015 it was the most downloaded npm package with over 1 billion downloads. Version 4 shipped in 2016 incorporating ideas from a proposed merge with Underscore. The project remains the dominant JavaScript utility library.
Lodash.summary_abuse: Lodash is a foundational JavaScript utility library with no server component or consumer-facing surface. It has never been implicated in meaningful abuse; the only security note is occasional CVEs in older versions due to prototype-pollution patterns, which have been patched in v4.17.21+.

Lodel.history: Developed by Cléo (now part of OpenEdition) since 2000 as an open-source CMS for scientific and academic publishing in humanities and social sciences. It is the backbone of the French OpenEdition platform hosting thousands of journals and books.
Lodel.summary_abuse: Lodel is a specialized academic publishing CMS used by French research institutions. It has no consumer-facing abuse surface and is not known to be exploited for malicious purposes.

Lodgify.history: Lodgify was founded in 2012 by Marco De Gregorio and Dennis Klett, headquartered in Barcelona, Spain. It raised $37.3M including a Series B in 2022. Lodgify provides website building, property management and channel management tools for vacation rental hosts.
Lodgify.summary_abuse: Lodgify is a vacation rental SaaS platform. Like all hospitality booking platforms, it could theoretically be used to advertise fake rentals, but it serves verified operators and requires payment processing. No notable fraud incidents documented.

Lofty.history: Formerly known as Chime Technologies, Lofty is an AI-powered real estate CRM and lead generation platform headquartered in Phoenix, AZ. It was rebranded from Chime to Lofty and expanded its AI capabilities with an agentic operating system for agents and brokerages.
Lofty.summary_abuse: Lofty is a B2B real estate CRM with no public-facing consumer content hosting. It handles real estate lead data and requires payment; abuse surface is limited to standard data privacy concerns around handling prospect data.

Logaholic.history: Logaholic is a Netherlands-based web analytics platform that combines log file analysis with JavaScript tracking and social media data. It supports self-hosted deployments offering full data ownership, differentiating from cloud-only analytics products.
Logaholic.summary_abuse: Logaholic is a self-hosted web analytics tool with no consumer-facing surface or hosted content. Negligible abuse risk.

Loggly.history: Founded in 2009 in San Francisco. Acquired by SolarWinds in January 2018 to strengthen their cloud monitoring portfolio alongside Papertrail, AppOptics and Pingdom. Loggly provides cloud-based log management and analytics as part of the SolarWinds product family.
Loggly.summary_abuse: Loggly is a log management SaaS owned by SolarWinds. It processes application log data uploaded by enterprise customers and has no public content-hosting surface. SolarWinds itself suffered a major supply-chain attack (SUNBURST) in 2020 which affected multiple products; Loggly was not the direct vector but the parent company's security posture was scrutinized.

LogiCommerce.history: LogiCommerce is a Spanish headless ecommerce platform developed 100% in Spain, founded around 2010 and based in Barcelona/Madrid. It serves B2B and B2C brands including VW, GAP, Audi and Nestlé with a unified platform for managing online stores, marketplaces, and physical stores.
LogiCommerce.summary_abuse: LogiCommerce is a B2B/B2C ecommerce platform serving large European brands. It requires merchant onboarding and does not host arbitrary public storefronts. No documented abuse incidents.

Login with Amazon.history: Launched by Amazon circa 2012 as an OAuth 2.0 single sign-on service for third-party websites and apps. It allows customers to use their Amazon account credentials to log in elsewhere, leveraging Amazon's existing identity platform.
Login with Amazon.summary_abuse: Login with Amazon is an OAuth SSO service backed by Amazon's identity infrastructure. It does not host arbitrary third-party content and benefits from Amazon's substantial security investment. Risk is primarily that phishing campaigns may attempt to spoof the login flow, but the vendor responds appropriately. No specific notable abuse incidents documented beyond standard phishing targeting Amazon credentials.

LoginRadius.history: Founded in 2012 in Vancouver, Canada by Rakesh Soni and Deepak Gupta. Raised $21.8M total including a $17M Series A led by Microsoft M12 and ForgePoint Capital. Recognized as a CIAM leader by Gartner and KuppingerCole. Now valued over $1 billion.
LoginRadius.summary_abuse: LoginRadius is a CIAM platform handling authentication for enterprise applications. It processes user identity data and SSO flows on behalf of enterprise customers. Standard risks involve credential stuffing and account takeover targeting its authentication layer, which LoginRadius mitigates through MFA and bot-detection features. No notable public breach documented.

Loglib.history: Loglib is an open-source, privacy-first web analytics alternative to Google Analytics and Mixpanel, created around 2023. It is self-hostable and GDPR compliant by default. The project has experienced reduced activity with reports of going 8+ months without updates.
Loglib.summary_abuse: Loglib is a privacy-focused analytics library with no consumer surface. The self-hosted option eliminates third-party data sharing. No documented abuse concerns.

LogoiX.history: LogoiX GmbH was founded in 2011 by former logistics managers from German forwarding companies, and is headquartered in Freilassing, Bavaria, near the Austrian border. The company provides cross-border logistics services, offering personal German delivery addresses and EU-wide affordable shipping for international buyers purchasing from German merchants.
LogoiX.summary_abuse: LogoiX is a German logistics carrier and parcel forwarding service with no documented abuse history. The parcel forwarding model carries a low residual risk of being used to receive fraudulently purchased goods, but LogoiX is a registered German GmbH operating in a regulated logistics environment and has no notable reports of misuse.

LogRocket.history: Founded in 2016 in Cambridge, MA by Matthew Arbesfeld and Ben Edelstein. Raised $51M total with a $25M Series C in 2022, backed by Battery Ventures and Matrix Partners. Serves 3,000+ companies including Reddit, Airbnb, and Bloomberg with session replay and product analytics.
LogRocket.summary_abuse: LogRocket records user sessions on client websites, which raises privacy considerations when not properly disclosed to end users. The vendor provides GDPR tooling including masking and consent mechanisms. The risk is primarily privacy-related rather than active abuse; the SDK itself is embedded server-side by the website owner.

Loja Integrada.history: Launched in 2012 in São Paulo, Brazil targeting micro and small businesses. Acquired by VTEX in September 2013. Hosts over 26,000 active stores, predominantly in the Brazilian market. Experienced a ~17% year-over-year decline in store count in 2025.
Loja Integrada.summary_abuse: Loja Integrada hosts thousands of small Brazilian e-commerce stores on its free and paid plans. Like any platform with free tiers, it could be used to stand up fake storefronts, but it integrates payment processors that provide some KYC friction. No major documented abuse incidents.

Loja Mestre.history: Loja Mestre is a Brazilian B2B e-commerce platform based in Curitiba, Paraná with over 20 years of operation. It offers a fixed monthly fee model without traffic charges and serves SMB sellers with B2B and B2C capabilities.
Loja Mestre.summary_abuse: Loja Mestre hosts Brazilian SMB e-commerce stores. It is primarily a B2B-focused platform with basic merchant onboarding. No notable documented abuse incidents.

Loja Virtual.history: Loja Virtual is a Brazilian e-commerce platform builder targeting small businesses and entrepreneurs in Brazil. Limited publicly available information on ownership or founding details.
Loja Virtual.summary_abuse: Loja Virtual is a small Brazilian e-commerce platform. As a low-cost or free store builder it could potentially host fraudulent storefronts, but serves a primarily legitimate SMB market. No specific documented abuse incidents.

Loja2.history: Loja2 is a Brazilian e-commerce platform founded in 2007 targeting small entrepreneurs. It focuses primarily on the Brazilian market and offers flexible pricing including a free plan.
Loja2.summary_abuse: Loja2 is a small Brazilian e-commerce platform. As a free-tier store builder it could potentially host fraudulent storefronts, though it primarily serves legitimate SMBs. No specific documented abuse incidents.

Lojas Online CTT.history: Lojas Online CTT is an e-commerce platform provided by CTT – Correios de Portugal, the Portuguese national postal service. The platform leverages CTT's logistics network to bundle website creation with shipping via CTT Expresso and Collectt pickup points.
Lojas Online CTT.summary_abuse: Lojas Online CTT is operated by Portugal's national postal service. Merchants are verified businesses and the platform integrates postal carrier services. Fraud risk is low given the operator's public-company accountability. No documented abuse incidents.

Loloyal.history: Loloyal (now rebranded to TrustWILL Loyalty by CWILL) is a Shopify loyalty and referral program app. The app was developed by a Chinese team and operates within the Shopify ecosystem providing points, VIP tiers and referral programs.
Loloyal.summary_abuse: Loloyal is a Shopify loyalty app with no public content hosting surface. Risk is limited to standard data handling concerns for customer purchase data shared with the app. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Looker.history: Founded in 2012 in Santa Cruz, CA by Lloyd Tabb and Ben Porterfield. Pioneered LookML, a data modeling language for semantic analytics layers over modern cloud databases. Acquired by Google for $2.6 billion in June 2019, completed February 2020. Now part of Google Cloud Platform.
Looker.summary_abuse: Looker is an enterprise BI platform owned by Google. It processes structured business data within customer-controlled cloud environments and has no public content-hosting surface. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Looksize.history: LookSize is a virtual fitting room SaaS that provides size recommendation widgets for fashion e-commerce stores. The platform connects to sizing charts from 3,000+ brands and helps shoppers find correct sizes. Founded around 2018.
Looksize.summary_abuse: LookSize is a B2B fashion tech widget that processes body measurements for size recommendations. It does not host user-generated content and has no direct consumer abuse surface. No documented abuse incidents.

Loom.history: Founded in 2016 by Vinay Hiremath, Shahed Khan, and Joe Thomas. Grew to 25 million users as an async video messaging tool. Acquired by Atlassian for approximately $975 million in November 2023 and integrated into Jira, Confluence and other Atlassian products.
Loom.summary_abuse: Loom is a video messaging platform that hosts user-recorded screen and webcam videos. The free tier allows shareable video links which could be abused to distribute harmful content or phishing demos. Atlassian provides content moderation and abuse reporting. No major documented abuse campaigns documented specifically targeting Loom's infrastructure.

Loop Returns.history: Loop Returns is a US-based ecommerce returns and operations management platform founded in 2017 in Columbus, OH. Backed by Shopify, FirstMark, and Lerer Hippeau with $175M+ raised. Acquired Wonderment in 2024. Serves 3,500+ brands processing 40M+ returns annually.
Loop Returns.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Well-funded ecommerce returns platform with strong brand adoption and legitimate operations.

Loop Web.history: Loop (loop.software) is a UK estate agency software and CRM platform founded in 2017 in Moreton-in-Marsh, England by Mark Bevan. It provides residential sales, lettings, property management, and website templates for independent UK estate agents. Pricing is £149/month flat with no setup fees.
Loop Web.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Niche UK estate agency software with transparent pricing and a clear professional customer base.

Loop54.history: Loop54 is an AI-powered ecommerce site search and product recommendation engine founded in 2010 in Stockholm, Sweden by Mike Odin. Raised $580K seed in 2015. Acquired by FactFinder on October 22, 2021. Notable clients included Coop.se, Telia, Office Depot, and Siba.
Loop54.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. A legitimate ecommerce search SaaS that was acquired by FactFinder in 2021 and operates as part of that enterprise.

LoopedIn.history: LoopedIn (formerly Productstash) is a UK-based product feedback and roadmap management tool founded around 2020 by Reuben. Rebranded and expanded to cover changelogs, knowledge bases and customer communication widgets. Sold via AppSumo lifetime deals.
LoopedIn.summary_abuse: No notable abuse concerns. Small UK SaaS product for feedback and roadmap management with clean track record.

Loopi.history: Loopi (loopi.org) appears to be a small SaaS platform focused on digital review cards and reputation management for small businesses, allowing customers to leave testimonials and manage online presence. Limited public information available; appears to be a micro-SaaS.
Loopi.summary_abuse: Limited information available. Small niche review/reputation management tool with no notable abuse history found.

Loops.history: Loops (loops.so) is an email platform for SaaS companies founded in 2022 by Chris Frantz and Adam Kaczmarek. YC W22 alumni. Raised $3.2M seed funding. Free tier includes 1,000 contacts and 4,000 sends/month. Focused on transactional and marketing email for startups.
Loops.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Legitimate YC-backed email platform for SaaS companies with transparent pricing.

Lootly.history: Lootly is a customer loyalty, referral, and affiliate marketing platform for ecommerce brands, founded in 2018 in Orlando, FL by Ryan Haidinger (CEO) and Larry VanDenHandel (CTO). Launched from TrustSpot as a separate loyalty product. Serves DTC brands needing loyalty and referral programs.
Lootly.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Legitimate loyalty and referral platform for ecommerce businesses with standard terms of service.

Loox.history: Loox is a Shopify product reviews platform serving over 100,000 merchants. It focuses on photo and video reviews and UGC syndication to Google Shopping, Meta and TikTok. Founded around 2015.
Loox.summary_abuse: Loox collects product reviews and user-generated photos from shoppers. Fake review injection is a potential abuse vector but Loox operates within Shopify's review verification ecosystem. A 2026 merchant report noted a referral code bug but no broader abuse pattern is documented.

Loqate.history: Founded in 2009 in San Mateo, California, specializing in global address verification and location intelligence. Acquired by UK-listed GBG plc for $13.4M, expanding GBG's global data quality and identity verification capabilities. Now operates as GBG Loqate.
Loqate.summary_abuse: Loqate is an address verification API used for fraud prevention and KYC workflows. It is not a surface that attackers exploit — rather, it is used to detect fraud. No documented abuse incidents.

LotLinx.history: LotLinx is a US automotive advertising technology company founded around 2012. It pioneered VIN-specific inventory advertising for car dealerships, syndicating inventory to Google, Facebook and automotive portals. The platform uses AI and machine learning for shopper targeting.
LotLinx.summary_abuse: LotLinx is a B2B automotive advertising platform serving car dealerships. It processes advertising data and behavioral targeting signals but does not host user-generated content. No documented abuse incidents.

LottieFiles.history: LottieFiles was founded in 2017 in Malaysia by Kshitij Minglani and Nattu Adnan, built around the Lottie animation format created by Airbnb. Raised $9M Series A in 2021 led by M12 (Microsoft) and $37M Series B in 2022. Used by Google, TikTok, Disney and Netflix with 9M+ users.
LottieFiles.summary_abuse: LottieFiles is a legitimate animation hosting platform. Lottie animation files can potentially embed obfuscated JavaScript via dotLottie format — minor supply-chain concern. No significant platform abuse reports.

LOU.history: LOU is a Digital Adoption Platform (DAP) focused on user onboarding and in-app guidance for SaaS products. Founded around 2020, it competes with tools like Appcues and Userflow in the user onboarding space. The company targets product teams who want to add interactive guides and tooltips to their web applications without engineering effort.
LOU.summary_abuse: LOU is a user onboarding platform that adds overlays and guided tours to web applications. It processes behavioral data about user interactions for the purposes of onboarding analytics. Low abuse risk profile as it operates in the B2B SaaS space. No notable security incidents documented.

Lovable.history: Lovable (formerly GPT Engineer) was founded in 2023 in Stockholm, Sweden by Anton Osika and Fabian Hedin. Launched as an AI-powered full-stack app builder in 2024. Raised $200M Series A in July 2025 at $1.8B valuation and $330M Series B at $6.6B valuation in 2026.
Lovable.summary_abuse: Lovable is an AI app builder with legitimate use cases. Risk of AI-generated code being used to create phishing or malware scaffolding. No major platform abuse reports.

Lovi.history: Lovi is an AI-powered skincare app founded in 2021 in Limassol, Cyprus by Alexey Shagraev and Tonya Zhukova. It offers cosmetic ingredient scanning, face analysis, and personalized skincare recommendations. Raised $550K in seed funding in 2025.
Lovi.summary_abuse: Lovi is a consumer-facing mobile app with no notable abuse history. Primary concern is health data privacy from face scans and product preferences.

Lovingly.history: Lovingly was founded in 2008 by Joe Vega and Ken Garland in New York. Started as a two-person operation building ecommerce tech for local florists, growing organically for over a decade. Now serves 1500+ florist partners across the US.
Lovingly.summary_abuse: Lovingly is a niche floral ecommerce platform with no documented abuse incidents. Low risk platform focused on local florist e-commerce.

Loyalis.history: Loyalis is a Dutch insurer founded in 2002 in Heerlen, originally owned by APG Groep (pension administrator). In 2019 acquired by a.s.r. Nederland for 450M EUR. Provides disability, survivors and supplementary pension insurance to 450,000 insured persons at 10,000 employers.
Loyalis.summary_abuse: Loyalis is a regulated Dutch insurance company supervised by DNB. No web-based abuse risk. No documented security incidents.

LoyaltyLion.history: LoyaltyLion was founded in 2012 in London by Dave Clark and Charlie Casey. It provides a data-driven loyalty and engagement platform for ecommerce merchants. Raised $12.5M Series A from Kennet Partners in March 2021 and $12M growth round from SaaS Capital in September 2022.
LoyaltyLion.summary_abuse: LoyaltyLion is a legitimate ecommerce loyalty platform with no documented abuse incidents. Collects customer purchase and behavior data for loyalty programs.

LoyaltyLoop.history: Originally founded in 2001 as SurveyAdvantage, evolved into CustomerPulse by 2006 as one of the first NPS feedback platforms. New ownership acquired the company in 2016 and relaunched as LoyaltyLoop. Now an all-in-one customer feedback and review platform targeting SMBs.
LoyaltyLoop.summary_abuse: LoyaltyLoop is a small customer feedback platform with no documented abuse incidents. Low-risk niche tool for SMBs.

Loyalzoo.history: Loyalzoo was founded in 2013 in London (Chiswick) as a digital loyalty card service for SMB retailers. It integrates directly with POS systems. Raised $890K including crowdfunding rounds in 2014 and 2015. In 2026 partnered with Priority for POS platform integration.
Loyalzoo.summary_abuse: Loyalzoo is a small UK-based POS loyalty platform with no documented abuse incidents. Very low risk product serving SMBs.

Loyoly.history: Loyoly (formerly Wiink) was founded in 2021 in Nantes, France by Joseph Aubry, Thomas Guibert and Thierry Girod. Launched on Shopify in January 2023. Provides loyalty, referral, and UGC reward programs with social media auto-verification. Raised approximately 1M EUR.
Loyoly.summary_abuse: Loyoly is a small French Shopify loyalty app with no documented abuse incidents. Low risk product targeting Shopify merchants.

Lozad.js.history: Lozad.js is an open-source lazy loading JavaScript library created by Apoorv Saxena in 2017. It uses the IntersectionObserver API to lazy-load images, iframes and other HTML elements with no dependencies. Weighs ~1kb minified and gzipped.
Lozad.js.summary_abuse: Lozad.js is a minimal, pure JavaScript lazy-loading library. No abuse risk. Open source with MIT license.

LPQV.history: LPQV is a Brazilian ecommerce and landing page platform for online stores. No notable history events documented.
LPQV.summary_abuse: LPQV is a small Brazilian ecommerce and landing page platform with no notable abuse signal documented.

LTheme.history: LTheme is a theme provider for WordPress and Joomla, offering premium website themes for the popular open-source CMS platforms. The company appears to be a small boutique theme shop, likely based in Vietnam or Southeast Asia, selling themes primarily through their own website and the Envato marketplace.
LTheme.summary_abuse: LTheme sells website themes for WordPress and Joomla. Theme shops have historically been a vector for malicious code injection in pirated theme distributions on unauthorized sites. However, themes purchased directly from LTheme represent no direct hosting or service abuse risk. Customers should verify they obtain themes from official channels to avoid nulled or backdoored versions.

Lua.history: Lua is a lightweight, embeddable scripting language created in 1993 by Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo, and Waldemar Celes at PUC-Rio in Brazil. Widely used in game engines (Roblox, World of Warcraft), embedded systems, and web servers like Nginx via OpenResty.
Lua.summary_abuse: Lua is a legitimate general-purpose scripting language with no inherent abuse characteristics. Used in games, embedded systems, and servers. No notable supply-chain risks.

Luana.history: Luana AI is an AI-powered financial analytics platform based in Palo Alto, CA, targeting FinTech, banking, and insurance finance teams. It connects to ERPs and financial systems to create a finance data lake with conversational AI and flexible reporting. Early-stage with 1-10 employees.
Luana.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Early-stage B2B fintech analytics tool for finance teams with no notable negative history.

Lucene.history: Apache Lucene is an open-source full-text search library written in Java. Originally created by Doug Cutting in 1999 and donated to Apache in 2001. Forms the foundation for Elasticsearch, Solr and OpenSearch. Industry standard for search indexing.
Lucene.summary_abuse: Apache Lucene is a foundational search library governed by Apache Software Foundation. No abuse risk. Used in countless enterprise search applications.

Lucide.history: Lucide is an open-source icon library and community fork of Feather Icons. Provides 1,600+ clean SVG icons available as React, Vue, Svelte and other framework components. MIT licensed and community maintained via Open Collective.
Lucide.summary_abuse: Lucide is a popular open-source icon library with no abuse risk. Community-maintained fork of Feather Icons.

Lucky Orange.history: Lucky Orange is a bootstrapped website analytics platform founded in 2010 (or 2012) in Overland Park, Kansas, US. Provides heatmaps, session recordings, live chat, and conversion funnels. Reached $2M ARR with 15K customers without external funding.
Lucky Orange.summary_abuse: Lucky Orange is a session recording tool that installs visitor tracking scripts. Potential privacy concern as it records visitor behavior including keystrokes in form fields if misconfigured. No documented data breach incidents.

Luigi’s Box.history: The team started researching search/personalization in 2014 and officially launched Luigi’s Box in 2016. It remains independently run from Bratislava, with no notable acquisitions or deprecation events found.
Luigi’s Box.summary_abuse: Luigi’s Box has little abuse signal beyond ordinary SaaS misuse. It is an e-commerce search and discovery platform, not a hosting or messaging service, and it publishes a bounty program plus responsible-disclosure reporting with defined response times and payouts.

Luma.history: Luma is an event management platform founded in April 2020 in San Francisco by Dan Liu and Victor Pontis. It enables event creators to set up pages, sell tickets, and manage attendees. Raised $3M seed from Maven Ventures and Venrock in December 2020.
Luma.summary_abuse: Luma is a legitimate events platform with low abuse risk. No documented significant incidents.

Lume.history: Lume is an open-source static site generator for Deno created by Óscar Otero around 2020. Supports Markdown, YAML, JSX, TypeScript, Vento, and Nunjucks templates. Deno-native, no node_modules. V2 released with Vento as default template engine.
Lume.summary_abuse: Lume is an open-source SSG for Deno with no abuse risk. Generates static files locally, no server-side components.

Lumino.history: Lumino (formerly PhosphorJS) is a TypeScript library for building interactive desktop-like web applications, used as the foundation of JupyterLab. Maintained by the Jupyter Foundation. Provides widgets, layouts, events and data structures for high-performance web apps.
Lumino.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. A well-maintained, BSD-licensed open-source library from the Jupyter Foundation, widely used in scientific computing and data science.

Lumio.history: Lumio is a cloud-based interactive learning platform by SMART Technologies (Canada), a company known for SMART Boards. It enables teachers to create and deliver engaging lessons with integrations for Desmos, Canvas, Schoology, and other LMS platforms. AI Assist launched in 2025.
Lumio.summary_abuse: Lumio is an EdTech platform by SMART Technologies. No known abuse incidents. Collects student interaction data which requires careful FERPA/COPPA compliance for school use.

Lumise.history: Lumise is a product designer plugin for WooCommerce/WordPress and Shopify created by King-Theme, an Envato Elite Author. Allows customers to customize products (t-shirts, mugs, phone cases) with a visual design editor. Sold as a one-time license on CodeCanyon and as a Shopify app.
Lumise.summary_abuse: Lumise is a WooCommerce/Shopify product designer plugin with no documented abuse incidents. Low-risk niche tool for print-on-demand stores.

Lumit.history: Lumit (lumit.io) is a quality inspection and process management software platform serving industrial sectors including coating and footwear. Offers products like CoatWeightInspekt and ProdInspekt. Limited public information; niche industrial B2B software provider.
Lumit.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Niche industrial inspection software with minimal web presence and no known issues.

Luna.history: Luna was formed in 2022 as a standalone B2B business under common ownership with 1-800 Contacts, combining Ditto, 6over6, Premium Vision, and ExpressExam. Its roots go back to Ditto’s 2011 virtual try-on work and 6over6’s 2014 prescription technology, with the brand unifying those lines in 2022.
Luna.summary_abuse: Luna has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B vision platform for eyewear retailers, brands, and doctors rather than a consumer-facing hosting or marketing tool. The main sensitivity is that it processes facial images and optical data for virtual try-on and prescription workflows, but the public materials frame it as a controlled enterprise service with privacy terms and limited public self-service surface.

Lunio.history: Lunio (formerly PPC Protect) was founded in 2016 in Chorley, England by Neil Andrew, Segev Hochberg, and Alex Winston. It protects digital ad budgets by identifying and excluding fake non-human traffic across 15+ ad networks including Google, Facebook, and LinkedIn. Raised $15M Series A in September 2022.
Lunio.summary_abuse: Lunio is a legitimate ad fraud detection platform. No notable abuse incidents. The platform itself is used to combat abuse rather than enable it.

Lunr.js.history: Lunr.js is a small, full-text search library for use in the browser and Node.js. Created by Oliver Nightingale around 2013. Allows adding fast and powerful search capabilities to static sites without a server-side index. Inspired by Solr.
Lunr.js.summary_abuse: Lunr.js is a client-side search library with no abuse risk. Open source MIT licensed JavaScript library.

Luveedu Cloud.history: Luveedu Cloud is an Indian web hosting and cloud services platform founded April 24, 2018 in Tripura by Ariyan Debnath. A subsidiary of WebXenith Technologies LLP. Offers shared hosting, cloud hosting, VPS, CDN, DNS, SSL, and DDoS protection using Google Cloud and AWS infrastructure.
Luveedu Cloud.summary_abuse: No serious abuse concerns. Small Indian hosting provider with limited market presence. Standard hosting service with no notable negative history.

Lychee.history: Lychee is a free self-hosted photo management system originally created by Tobias Reich (electerious) around 2014. The LycheeOrg community organization took over active development when the original project slowed. Built with PHP/Laravel, it allows users to upload, organize, and share photos from their own server.
Lychee.summary_abuse: Lychee is a self-hosted photo management tool with no abuse risk. All data stays on the user's own server.

LyraThemes Kale.history: Kale is a free food and lifestyle blog WordPress theme by LyraThemes, a Texas-based WordPress theme company established around 2016. LyraThemes offers free versions of all premium themes; Kale Pro is the paid upgrade at $39 one-time. Popular with food bloggers and lifestyle bloggers.
LyraThemes Kale.summary_abuse: No abuse concerns. Standard WordPress freemium theme from a small Texas-based theme shop with clean track record.

Lytics.history: Lytics was founded in 2011 in Portland, Oregon as a customer data platform (CDP). Raised $58.3M over 6 rounds. In January 2025 was acquired by Contentstack, a headless CMS company, to enhance its composable digital experience platform.
Lytics.summary_abuse: Lytics is a legitimate enterprise CDP now owned by Contentstack. Handles customer behavioral data — requires careful GDPR/CCPA compliance. No documented security incidents.
